proposals.title: Geopolitical Fragmentation and Land-Use Futures: Modeling Deforestation and Agricultural Expansion under Competing Socio-Environmental Regimes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 global sustainability governance and supply chain models often assume a cooperative, globalized society moving toward unified environmental standards. However, rising geopolitical tensions, trade bloc formation, and divergent national policies (e.g., EUDR vs. alternative standards) challenge this assumption. Understanding how land-use change and commodity flows adapt to a world of fragmented, competing societal blocs with different sustainability thresholds is critical for realistic policy and conservation planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that geopolitical fragmentation into blocs with distinct sustainability regulations and trade policies will redirect commodity flows, displace deforestation frontiers, and create new leakage patterns that are not captured by models assuming global cooperation.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) coupled with a spatially explicit land-use change model. The ABM will represent major trading blocs (e.g., EU, US, China-aligned) as agents with defined sustainability thresholds and trade rules. Land-use decisions by sub-national producer agents will be influenced by these bloc-specific market access and price premiums. We will use convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on Sentinel-2 and Landsat imagery (pre-2026 state-of-the-art) to historically map commodity-driven deforestation, training the model to project future patterns under different fragmentation scenarios from 2025-2040.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for assessing the land-system consequences of deglobalization, moving beyond single-supply-chain analyses. It will quantify the risk of policy-driven deforestation leakage and identify regions most vulnerable to shifting commodity demands. The results will inform the design of more resilient transnational governance and due-diligence policies that account for geopolitical realities.
proposals.novelty: This integrates Lambin's expertise in value chain drivers of land use with a geopolitical scenario framework, explicitly relaxing the ubiquitous assumption of a unified global society in sustainability transitions literature. The coupling of a geopolitical bloc ABM with deep learning-based land-use projection is a methodological advance.

proposals.title: Harnessing Path Dependencies in Agricultural Value Chains to Accelerate Deforestation-Free Transitions
proposals.motivation: Path dependency in agricultural commodity systems, such as cocoa and palm oil, is widely recognized as a major barrier to sustainability, locking in deforestation-linked practices. However, pre-2026 research, including Lambin's work, focuses on these lags as problems to be overcome, not as potential levers. If existing socio-technical and institutional pathways can be strategically redirected, they could provide a faster route to sustainability than building entirely new systems from scratch.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, pre-existing path dependencies within agricultural commodity value chains—such as established farmer-buyer networks, entrenched certification infrastructures, or dominant crop varieties—can be identified and strategically repurposed to accelerate the adoption of deforestation-free practices, rather than inherently slowing it down.
proposals.method: We will combine satellite time-series analysis (using Sentinel-2) with value chain actor surveys in contrasting case regions (e.g., cocoa in Ecuador, palm oil in Indonesia). A deep learning model (specifically, a U-Net architecture for semantic segmentation) will be trained on pre-2026 data to map historical land-use trajectories and identify stable, path-dependent agricultural footprints. These geospatial clusters will be linked via spatial regression to survey data on value chain factors (e.g., buyer contracts, input supply, extension services) to quantify which existing dependencies correlate with rapid vs. slow adoption of sustainable practices.
proposals.contribution: This research will move the field beyond diagnosing path dependency as a barrier to empirically testing it as a potential accelerator. It will provide a novel, actionable framework for policymakers and corporations to 'bend' existing value chain structures toward sustainability goals. The integrated geospatial-value chain methodology will offer a new tool for targeting interventions where they are most likely to catalyze rapid, systemic change.
proposals.novelty: While path dependency is seen as a source of inertia, this study innovatively investigates its latent potential for positive leverage. It uniquely operationalizes this by linking deep learning-derived land-use path maps with fine-grained value chain actor data to identify specific, repurposable dependencies.

proposals.title: Scaling Niche Regimes for Deforestation-Free Commodities: A Value Chain and Geospatial Analysis of Institutional Resource Flows
proposals.motivation: Despite the proliferation of niche innovations (e.g., community-led agroforestry, certified sustainable sourcing) aimed at halting deforestation, their impact remains limited without integration into mainstream institutional frameworks and financing. This disconnect between viable local alternatives and the broader policy and market structures is a critical bottleneck in achieving deforestation-free supply chains, as identified in pre-2026 research. Understanding the specific processes that enable or hinder this scaling is essential for effective land-use governance.
proposals.hypothesis: The successful linkage of niche sustainable land-use regimes to wider institutional resources is non-random and can be predicted by specific configurations of value chain governance, the demonstrable environmental performance of the niche, and the presence of bridging organizations that translate local evidence into policy-relevant formats.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-scalar, mixed methods. First, using pre-2026 deep learning architectures (specifically, U-Net models for semantic segmentation of Sentinel-2 and PlanetScope imagery), we will map and longitudinally track the biophysical outcomes (e.g., forest cover, aquatic vegetation, crop patterns) of identified niche regimes in case study landscapes. Second, we will conduct structured surveys and network analysis with actors along the commodity value chain (farmers, cooperatives, traders, NGOs, policymakers) to quantify resource flows, governance structures, and information pathways. The geospatial and socio-economic data will be analyzed using statistical matching and pathway analysis to identify causal configurations linking niche performance to institutional support.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, evidence-based framework for diagnosing scaling bottlenecks in sustainable land-use transitions. It will advance the field of land system science by empirically connecting micro-level socio-ecological practices with macro-level institutional economics, moving beyond case-study description. The integrated methodology offers a replicable toolkit for policymakers and value chain actors to strategically target investments and partnerships.
proposals.novelty: While niche regimes and value chain factors are studied separately, this proposal innovatively fuses high-resolution remote sensing of environmental performance with value chain network analysis to objectively trace and explain the flow of institutional resources, a process typically described only qualitatively.

proposals.title: Leveraging Value Chain Data and Satellite Monitoring to Design Targeted Incentives for Private-Sector Innovation in Deforestation-Free Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: Despite corporate zero-deforestation commitments, implementation gaps persist due to misaligned incentives for upstream suppliers and insufficient innovation in sustainable practices. Pre-2026 research, including Lambin's work, shows that value chain structures and governance critically influence farmer adoption, but policy mechanisms to directly spur private-sector innovation remain under-designed. This limits the scalability of truly sustainable commodity production.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted, data-driven incentive mechanisms that integrate value chain actor relationships with high-resolution monitoring of on-farm innovation can significantly increase the adoption and effectiveness of private-sector sustainability innovations in agricultural supply chains.
proposals.method: The research will combine value chain analysis (surveys, network mapping) with remote sensing. We will train a multi-temporal U-Net convolutional neural network on Sentinel-2 and PlanetScope imagery to segment and classify fine-scale agricultural practices (e.g., agroforestry, irrigation infrastructure) adopted by farmers within a target commodity chain (e.g., cocoa in West Africa). This geospatial data on innovation adoption will be linked, via farmer and cooperative IDs, to detailed value chain data on contracts, premiums, and technical assistance. A quasi-experimental design will then evaluate how different incentive structures, identified from the chain analysis, correlate with the spatial diffusion and ecological impact of innovations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for designing spatially explicit, chain-specific incentive mechanisms, moving beyond blanket commitments. It will empirically demonstrate how coupling value chain governance data with satellite-derived measures of practice adoption can improve the targeting and accountability of sustainability investments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work separates chain analysis from land monitoring, this integrates them to directly model the causal link between specific incentive structures (the policy lever) and observable on-the-ground innovation (the outcome), offering a precision tool for policy design.

proposals.title: A Multi-Level Perspective on the Transition to Deforestation-Free Commodity Value Chains: Integrating Remote Sensing and Actor Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Despite proliferating zero-deforestation commitments, the implementation of sustainable practices in commodity supply chains remains inconsistent and poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Lambin and others highlights the critical role of value chain factors but lacks a systemic framework to analyze how niche innovations, incumbent regime pressures, and broader landscape trends interact to enable or hinder sustainability transitions. This gap limits the design of effective policies.
proposals.hypothesis: The successful transition to deforestation-free commodity systems requires a specific, synergistic alignment of pressures from the socio-technical landscape, destabilization of the incumbent agricultural regime, and the empowerment of niche innovations through value chain support structures.
proposals.method: We will apply the Multi-Level Perspective (MLP) framework to a comparative case study of cocoa in Ecuador and palm oil in Indonesia. The analysis integrates: 1) Actor network mapping and policy document analysis to characterize the regime and landscape levels; 2) In-depth surveys and interviews with farmers and chain actors to assess niche practice adoption and value chain linkages; 3) Time-series analysis of Sentinel-2 and Landsat 8 imagery using a U-Net convolutional neural network architecture for semantic segmentation to objectively map land cover change and validate deforestation outcomes linked to the MLP factors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, integrated theoretical and empirical model of sustainability transitions in land-use systems, moving beyond factor identification to explain dynamic interactions. It will offer actionable insights for policymakers and corporations on leveraging specific MLP interactions to accelerate transitions, directly informing the debate on the reality of deforestation-free supply chains.
proposals.novelty: While the MLP is established in energy and mobility studies, its systematic application to agro-commodity deforestation, combined with the quantitative validation of transition pathways using state-of-the-art deep learning on satellite data, represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance for land system science.

proposals.title: Neighborhood Tipping Points: A Multi-Scale Analysis of Social-Ecological Transitions to Sustainable Land Use in Community-Managed Forests
proposals.motivation: Research on planetary boundaries and global tipping points has advanced, but the micro-scale dynamics of sustainability transitions remain poorly understood. Understanding if and how hyper-local social-ecological systems (SES) tip toward sustainable land use is critical for designing effective, community-based conservation and sustainable commodity production policies, which are central to deforestation-free supply chain goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neighborhood-scale SES exhibit identifiable tipping points toward sustainable forest management, triggered by specific configurations of local value chain incentives, community governance, and ecological feedbacks, which can be detected via high-resolution spatial and social data.
proposals.method: We will integrate Lambin's expertise in value chain analysis with advanced remote sensing. Using very high-resolution (e.g., Planet, Sentinel-2) satellite time series, we will train a U-Net convolutional neural network to segment and classify fine-scale land-use transitions (e.g., agroforestry adoption, forest regrowth) in community-managed landscapes in Ecuador and Uganda. This biophysical data will be coupled with spatially-explicit household surveys on value chain participation and farming practices. We will apply statistical techniques like panel regression and threshold models to identify non-linear relationships and tipping points between value chain factors, policy interventions, and land-cover outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically-grounded framework for analyzing sustainability transitions at the community scale. It will directly inform the implementation of 'zero-deforestation' supply chain policies by identifying the local leverage points that can trigger widespread adoption of sustainable practices. It bridges macro-scale tipping point theory with micro-scale land system science.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the conceptual framework of tipping point analysis to the hyper-local, community scale and in methodologically fusing deep learning-based fine-scale land change detection with spatially-referenced socio-economic data on value chain drivers.

proposals.title: Pathways to Permanence: A Value Chain and Land System Analysis of Positive Lock-Ins for Sustainable Land Use
proposals.motivation: Research on land-use change has extensively studied 'lock-in' to unsustainable pathways, such as deforestation frontiers, but offers limited systematic analysis of how socio-ecological systems become durably locked into *sustainable* trajectories. Understanding these positive lock-ins is critical for designing policies and interventions that can scale and persist beyond pilot projects, directly addressing global sustainability goals in agriculture and forestry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustainable land-use lock-ins emerge from specific, synergistic configurations of value chain governance (e.g., contracts, premiums), farmer practices, and landscape-scale reinforcing feedbacks (e.g., ecosystem service recovery, social learning), which can be identified through comparative case analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, multi-scale approach. First, we will use pre-2026 satellite imagery (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2) and a U-Net deep learning architecture to map and longitudinally track stable sustainable land covers (e.g., agroforestry, restored forests) in candidate regions. Second, we will conduct structured surveys and interviews with actors along the value chain (farmers, cooperatives, buyers) in selected cases to quantify the influence of chain factors (e.g., market access, technical support) on practice adoption. Third, we will use Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) to identify the necessary and sufficient conditions for sustained sustainability across cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework for analyzing sustainable lock-ins, moving beyond barrier-focused studies. It will generate empirical evidence on the combinatory role of value chain structures and local socio-ecological feedbacks, offering actionable insights for policymakers and corporations aiming to create durable deforestation-free commodity systems.
proposals.novelty: The study inverts the dominant analytical lens on lock-in from pathology to solution, and innovatively integrates deep learning-based land system mapping with value chain analysis and QCA to explain the emergence of durable sustainability.

proposals.title: Downscaling Non-Stationary Land Futures: A Value Chain-Integrated Framework for Robust Local Planning
proposals.motivation: Current land-use planning often relies on static downscaled projections, failing to capture the dynamic, non-stationary trajectories of land cover driven by climate change and socio-economic pressures. This mismatch creates significant risk for local policies on conservation, zoning, and infrastructure, which are vulnerable to unanticipated land transitions. There is an urgent need for downscaling methods that explicitly model uncertainty and dynamism to support adaptive management.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating value chain drivers and deep learning-based land cover segmentation into a probabilistic downscaling framework will generate more realistic ensembles of parcel-level land cover trajectories, significantly improving the robustness of local policy stress-testing compared to static projection methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel downscaling architecture that fuses coarse-resolution future scenarios (e.g., SSP-RCP) with high-resolution historical time-series from Sentinel-2, processed using a U-Net or similar deep learning model for precise land cover segmentation. Non-stationary transition probabilities will be modeled via Bayesian networks, explicitly parameterized with spatially explicit data on value chain factors (e.g., commodity prices, market access) and local policy levers. This will generate stochastic ensembles of fine-scale land cover trajectories, quantifying classification and transition uncertainty over decadal timescales.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a new methodological framework for dynamic, probabilistic downscaling that moves the field beyond static snapshots. It will deliver an open-source tool for local planners to visualize and stress-test policies against a range of plausible, non-stationary futures, directly linking global commodity drivers to local land outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely integrates the non-stationarity of land systems—driven by both biophysical and value-chain factors—into the core of the downscaling algorithm, producing ensembles of trajectories rather than a single deterministic map. This fusion of Lambin's expertise in value chain analysis with advanced deep learning for land cover change represents a significant advance in land system science.

proposals.title: Coupling Local Land-Use Decisions with Global Commodity Flows: A Multi-Scale Analysis of Scale Separation in Dryland Deforestation Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Sustainable land-use governance often assumes that local socio-ecological dynamics can be managed separately from global market forces, a concept known as scale separation. In drylands, where ecosystem resilience is low and livelihoods are precarious, this assumption is critical yet untested. The failure of many deforestation-free supply chain pledges, documented in pre-2026 research, suggests a fundamental misalignment between local actor decisions and global commodity chain pressures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in dryland socio-ecological systems, local land-use dynamics (e.g., smallholder agricultural expansion) and global commodity chain dynamics are not separable; instead, their coupling, mediated by value chain intermediaries and policies, is the primary driver of non-linear deforestation patterns.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite-derived land-cover change data (using a U-Net deep learning architecture for segmentation of Sentinel-2 imagery) with spatially explicit data on commodity value chains (e.g., cattle, cocoa) in a dryland case study region (e.g., the Cerrado or Sahel). A multi-scale statistical model (e.g., a hierarchical Bayesian network or an agent-based model coupled with system dynamics) will be calibrated to quantify the strength of feedbacks between local biophysical/social variables and global price/ demand signals. The model will test for the emergence of deforestation patterns that cannot be explained by local or global factors in isolation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical test of the scale separation concept in land system science. It will generate a novel methodological framework for coupling fine-scale remote sensing with value chain analysis. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective, multi-scale land governance and supply chain policies that account for cross-scale couplings.
proposals.novelty: While scale separation is a common implicit assumption, this study proposes its first direct, quantitative test in a dryland context by integrating Lambin's expertise in value chain analysis with state-of-the-art deep learning for land change detection, moving beyond descriptive case studies to causal inference.

proposals.title: Value Chain Governance and Human-Enhanced Resilience: A Multi-Scale Analysis of Dryland Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: Dominant narratives in land system science often frame human activity as a primary driver of dryland degradation. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests certain land-use practices, like well-managed agroforestry, may enhance ecosystem resilience. This research addresses a critical gap by systematically identifying where and under what governance conditions human activity in commodity value chains leads to improved dryland outcomes, moving beyond simplistic degradation assumptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in specific dryland socio-ecological systems, the integration of indigenous or sustainable agroforestry practices within value chains characterized by strong sustainability governance (e.g., specific contracts, premiums, technical support) leads to measurable increases in landscape resilience, as evidenced by vegetation stability and connectivity under climate variability.
proposals.method: We will combine satellite time-series analysis with value chain mapping. Using a U-Net deep learning architecture, we will segment high-resolution imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2) to map agroforestry patches and key resilience indicators (woody cover, soil vegetation) over time. This will be linked to household and trader surveys in selected cases (e.g., shea parklands in West Africa, silvopastoral systems in Latin America) to quantify value chain factors—market access, contract types, sustainability standards—identified in Lambin's prior work. A panel regression will model resilience metrics as a function of these chain factors and climatic stressors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, evidence-based typology of 'human-enhanced resilience' in drylands, directly informing the design of deforestation-free and resilience-building value chain policies. It will advance land system science by providing a methodological framework to rigorously test the net impact of socio-economic interventions on ecological resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the causal assumption of human impact by explicitly linking value chain governance—a socio-economic driver—to positive ecological resilience outcomes, using advanced remote sensing to move from case-study anecdotes to generalizable, spatially-explicit evidence.

proposals.title: Mapping Leakage and Synergy Pathways in Deforestation-Free Supply Chains: A Coupled System Analysis for Policy Design
proposals.motivation: The effectiveness of corporate zero-deforestation commitments and national policies is undermined by leakage, where deforestation is displaced to other regions or commodities, and by a lack of understanding of potential synergies with other sustainability goals. Pre-2026 research highlights these as critical failures in managing coupled human-environment systems, yet policy tools to systematically anticipate and mitigate these feedbacks remain underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating spatially explicit land-use change models with value chain network analysis will reveal specific, predictable pathways of leakage and synergy, enabling the design of targeted, multi-actor policy mixes that enhance the systemic impact of deforestation-free commitments.
proposals.method: We will combine two pre-2026 methodological strands. First, using Sentinel-2 and Landsat 8 imagery, we will train a U-Net convolutional neural network to map annual land-use change (2015-2025) for key commodity frontiers. Second, we will construct agent-based models of commodity value chains, parameterized with survey data from Lambin's prior work, to simulate actor decision-making. These components will be coupled within a simulation framework to test policy scenarios (e.g., jurisdictional approaches, conditional incentives) and quantify leakage/synergy effects on forest cover and farmer livelihoods.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, empirically grounded framework for ex-ante policy assessment in coupled systems. It will produce a typology of leakage pathways and synergy opportunities specific to different commodity chains and governance contexts, moving beyond case-study descriptions to generalizable insights for policymakers and supply chain actors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal coupling of deep learning-based land system science with agent-based value chain modeling to create a policy-screening tool, a synthesis not yet achieved in pre-2026 literature on sustainable supply chains.

proposals.title: Mapping the Telecoupled System: A Network Analysis of Social-Ecological Linkages in Deforestation-Free Supply Chain Governance
proposals.motivation: Despite corporate zero-deforestation commitments, leakage and non-compliance persist, revealing a critical gap in understanding the complex, multi-scale interactions between supply chain actors, local land-use decisions, and ecological outcomes. Pre-2026 research, including Lambin's work, identifies value chain factors as key but treats them in relative isolation, lacking a framework to model their systemic interdependence and feedbacks with biophysical variables.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deforestation outcomes in commodity frontiers are determined not by isolated factors, but by the structure and strength of connectivity within a telecoupled social-ecological network, where specific network configurations (e.g., centralization, brokerage) explain the efficacy or failure of sustainability governance.
proposals.method: We will construct multiplex networks for case study regions (e.g., cocoa in Ecuador, palm oil in Indonesia). Layers will integrate: 1) a social layer of value chain actors (farmers, traders, buyers) from survey data; 2) a policy/institutional layer from document analysis; and 3) an ecological layer of land-cover change and fragmentation from satellite imagery (using pre-trained CNNs like U-Net for segmentation). Network metrics (e.g., degree centrality, modularity, cross-layer correlation) will be calculated using Python's NetworkX and correlated with deforestation rates via statistical models to identify critical linkages and structural vulnerabilities.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, systemic diagnostic tool to identify leverage points and unintended consequences in sustainability governance. It will advance land system science by operationalizing the telecoupling framework with quantitative network methods, offering concrete insights for designing more resilient and effective deforestation-free supply chain interventions.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is used in social or ecological studies separately, its application to construct and analyze *multiplex* social-ecological networks for specific commodity systems is novel, moving beyond factor lists to model the structure of system linkages that drive land-use change.

proposals.title: From Global Signals to Local Shocks: A Multi-Scale Model of Climate-Market Teleconnections on Household Vulnerability in Tropical Commodity Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Global climate variability and commodity price shocks are key drivers of land-use change and rural livelihoods, but their impacts are mediated by complex, place-specific value chains and local environmental conditions. Pre-2026 research often treats these drivers at aggregate scales, creating a critical gap in understanding how they propagate to affect individual household vulnerability, a key determinant of deforestation risk and sustainable practice adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the propagation of global climate and market signals to household-scale vulnerability is non-linear and strongly moderated by the structure and governance of local agricultural value chains, which either amplify or buffer these external shocks.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-source data within a hierarchical modeling framework. First, we will use convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on Sentinel-2 and PlanetScope imagery to map fine-scale land cover and crop types. Second, we will construct a multi-layer teleconnection model linking global climate indices (e.g., ENSO) and commodity futures prices to regional market data. Third, using detailed household survey data from cocoa or palm oil systems, we will employ structural equation modeling to quantify how value chain factors (e.g., buyer contracts, certification, input access) moderate the impact of these downscaled signals on a composite vulnerability index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded framework for downscaling global socio-ecological drivers, directly linking macro-forces to micro-decision-making. It will advance the science of sustainable supply chains by identifying precise leverage points within value chains that can be targeted by policy to enhance household resilience and reduce deforestation pressure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, quantitative integration of remote sensing-derived land dynamics, global teleconnection models, and value chain analysis within a single causal framework focused on the household, moving beyond correlational studies at single scales.

proposals.title: Aggregating Local Forest Governance Successes: A Value Chain and Deep Learning Framework to Scale Up Deforestation-Free Practices for Regional Policy
proposals.motivation: Regional policies to curb deforestation often fail because they are not grounded in the diverse socio-economic and biophysical realities of local contexts. While successful local forest and farm management practices exist, there is no systematic, scalable method to identify, analyze, and aggregate these successes to inform higher-level policy design. This gap limits the effectiveness of regional land-use governance, particularly in commodity frontiers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating value chain analysis with satellite-derived land cover change data can systematically identify clusters of successful local adaptations (e.g., specific agroforestry or conservation practices), and that the common enabling factors across these clusters can be distilled into scalable policy principles for the region.
proposals.method: We will first use a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN), such as a U-Net architecture, on high-resolution satellite imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2) to map fine-scale land-use practices and forest cover stability over time in a target region (e.g., the Amazon or Congo Basin). Concurrently, we will conduct surveys and interviews along agricultural commodity value chains to map governance structures, market incentives, and farmer practices. We will then employ spatial clustering algorithms (e.g., DBSCAN) to identify geographic clusters where stable forest correlates with specific value chain configurations. A comparative case analysis will extract the shared institutional and economic factors enabling these local successes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, evidence-based methodology for 'bottom-up' policy design, moving beyond generic prescriptions. It will contribute a framework for policymakers to identify which local successes are scalable and under what regional value chain and tenure conditions. The work will advance the empirical assessment of 'deforestation-free' supply chains by linking pixel-level environmental outcomes to specific nodes in the value chain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of deep learning for detailed land-use segmentation with value chain political economy analysis to create a scalable diagnostic tool for policy. This moves beyond simply monitoring deforestation to explaining the local governance and market conditions that prevent it, and systematically aggregating those explanations.

proposals.title: Optimizing Livelihood Security under Biodiversity Constraints: A Value Chain and Land-Use Modeling Framework for Tropical Commodity Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Zero-deforestation and sustainability commitments in commodity supply chains often create trade-offs, potentially disadvantaging smallholder farmers and threatening rural livelihoods. A systematic framework to explicitly optimize for livelihood security while meeting biodiversity conservation targets is critically needed to move beyond binary deforestation narratives and support equitable, sustainable transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the dual problem of maximizing livelihood security subject to a biodiversity constraint, we hypothesize that spatially explicit interventions can be identified that outperform standard conservation-first approaches in achieving both socio-economic and ecological objectives in smallholder-dominated landscapes.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution land-use change analysis with value chain actor surveys in a cocoa or coffee-producing region. A convolutional neural network (CNN) will segment Sentinel-2 imagery to map land cover, crop types, and habitat quality as a biodiversity proxy. This spatial data will inform a multi-objective optimization model, using algorithms like NSGA-II, to identify Pareto-optimal frontiers between livelihood (modeled via farm-gate revenue and income stability from value chain data) and biodiversity outcomes under different policy and market scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, operationalizable framework for policymakers and companies to design spatially targeted interventions that reconcile livelihood and biodiversity goals. It will advance the empirical evaluation of 'sustainable' supply chains by quantifying trade-offs and synergies, directly contributing to debates on just sustainability transitions.
proposals.novelty: While trade-offs are well-studied, formally inverting the standard conservation optimization problem to prioritize livelihoods under a biodiversity constraint is novel. The integration of deep learning-derived habitat metrics with value chain socio-economic data within an optimization framework represents a significant methodological advance for land system science.

proposals.title: Mapping the Hidden Footprint: A Multi-System Assessment of Indirect Land-Use and Livelihood Impacts in Deforestation-Free Commodity Chains
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, corporate zero-deforestation commitments focus on direct supply chain impacts, potentially displacing environmental damage to other regions (leakage) and creating hidden social costs or benefits. A systematic, spatially explicit assessment of these indirect and spillover effects across sending, receiving, and broader landscape systems is critical to evaluate the true sustainability of these policies. This gap limits the ability of policymakers and companies to design interventions that are genuinely net-positive for people and ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deforestation-free supply chain policies, while reducing direct forest loss in sourcing regions, generate significant, quantifiable indirect land-use changes and heterogeneous livelihood impacts in spillover and receiving systems, with the net sustainability outcome depending on value chain structure and local governance.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-temporal satellite imagery (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2) with a spatially explicit panel dataset of land cover, value chain actors, and household surveys. Using a Difference-in-Differences framework combined with propensity score matching, we will isolate the causal impact of policy adoption in specific commodity chains (e.g., cocoa, palm oil). A convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, specifically a U-Net model pre-trained for land cover segmentation, will be deployed to detect fine-scale land-use changes in spillover zones, linking these patterns to value chain factors via agent-based modeling.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, integrated framework to quantify the hidden sustainability footprint of commodity supply chains, moving beyond direct sourcing. It will deliver empirical evidence on the systemic trade-offs and synergies between conservation and livelihoods, offering actionable insights for designing more effective and equitable land-use policies.
proposals.novelty: While leakage is acknowledged, this study uniquely integrates deep learning-based land change detection with value chain analysis and household-level socio-economic data to causally attribute spillover effects to specific chain actors and policies, a methodological advance beyond correlational landscape studies.

proposals.title: Asymmetry in Drivers: A Value Chain and Deep Learning Analysis of Deforestation and Forestation in Tropical Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Policies promoting forest restoration and sustainable supply chains often assume that the factors causing deforestation (e.g., weak governance, market pressures) are simply reversed to enable forestation. This assumption is untested and risks ineffective policy. Grounded in pre-2026 debates on zero-deforestation commitments and forest transitions, this research addresses a critical gap in understanding the distinct socio-economic and biophysical drivers of forest loss versus gain.
proposals.hypothesis: The key drivers of deforestation (e.g., corruption, commodity prices, infrastructure) are not symmetric to the drivers of forestation; forestation is instead primarily driven by a different set of factors related to local livelihood diversification, specific value chain incentives for sustainable practices, and biophysical preconditions.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite time-series analysis with socio-economic surveys. First, using a U-Net deep learning architecture (as in Lambin's 2025 work), we will segment annual Landsat/Sentinel-2 imagery (2000-2025) for selected tropical regions to create high-resolution maps of deforestation and forestation events. Second, we will spatially link these events to panel data on value chain factors (e.g., buyer contracts, certification, farmer cooperatives), governance indicators, and household surveys. Third, we will employ asymmetric statistical modeling (e.g., separate logistic regressions and machine learning classifiers like Random Forest) to identify and compare the significant drivers for loss versus gain.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence challenging the symmetry assumption, offering a more nuanced framework for land-use change science. It will generate practical insights for designing targeted policies: interventions to halt deforestation may differ from those needed to actively promote forestation, especially within specific commodity supply chains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes deforestation or forestation separately, this research directly tests for asymmetry in their drivers by combining Lambin's expertise in value chain analysis with state-of-the-art (pre-2026) deep learning segmentation, a novel methodological integration for this question.

proposals.title: Watershed-Scale Forestation Index: Linking Reforestation Patterns to Local Hydrological and Soil Ecosystem Services
proposals.motivation: Global forestation indices often fail to capture the local ecological and socio-economic benefits of reforestation, which are critical for community buy-in and sustainable land-use policy. A watershed is a fundamental ecological unit where forest cover directly regulates key services like water quality, sediment retention, and flood mitigation. Pre-2026, there is a gap in systematically linking fine-scale reforestation patterns within watersheds to quantifiable, local ecosystem service outcomes, hindering the design of targeted, effective restoration policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a forestation index framework at the watershed scale will reveal strong, quantifiable associations between specific spatial patterns of reforestation (e.g., riparian buffers vs. upland patches) and measurable improvements in local hydrological and soil retention services.
proposals.method: We will adapt Lambin's forestation index framework to watersheds using high-resolution (e.g., Sentinel-2) satellite imagery and a U-Net deep learning architecture for precise land cover segmentation, trained on regional data. For selected watersheds in tropical agricultural landscapes, we will analyze multi-temporal imagery (2015-2025) to map reforestation patterns. These patterns will be statistically linked to spatially explicit proxies for ecosystem services derived from remote sensing, such as the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) for biomass and the Modified Normalized Difference Water Index (MNDWI) for surface water turbidity, controlling for biophysical and value chain factors (e.g., market access, certification) via regression models.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a scalable, spatially explicit method to evaluate the local effectiveness of reforestation and forest conservation initiatives. It will contribute to land system science by bridging landscape ecology with supply chain governance, offering evidence-based insights for designing 'deforestation-free' commodity policies that genuinely enhance watershed resilience and local livelihoods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit downscaling of a forestation assessment framework to the ecologically coherent watershed unit and the integration of deep learning-based land cover change detection with spatially modeled ecosystem service proxies to test the local reality of sustainability claims in commodity-producing regions.

proposals.title: Leakage or Leverage? A Global Value Chain Analysis of Land-Use Displacement Associated with National Reforestation Policies
proposals.motivation: Many countries have ambitious reforestation pledges to meet climate and biodiversity goals. However, pre-2026 research on agricultural commodity supply chains suggests that local conservation can displace deforestation and degradation to other regions, undermining global net benefits. This study directly tests whether national-scale reforestation leads to such leakage or if it genuinely reduces aggregate pressure on forests worldwide.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national reforestation, particularly when driven by policy targets, is associated with increased imports of forest-risk commodities and/or intensified land degradation within the reforesting country, thereby displacing rather than eliminating environmental pressure.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution land-cover change data (e.g., Hansen GFC, ESA CCI) with global agricultural trade databases (FAOSTAT, COMTRADE) for 2001-2023. For countries with significant net reforestation, we will use panel regression models to test for correlations between reforested area and changes in imports of key commodities (e.g., soy, palm oil, beef). Concurrently, we will apply a pre-trained deep learning segmentation model (U-Net architecture on Sentinel-2 imagery) to detect subtle, intra-national degradation (e.g., selective logging, biomass loss) in remaining forests, testing if degradation rates increase post-reforestation.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first global, quantitative evidence linking national reforestation to potential land-use displacement via trade and domestic degradation. It will advance the field of telecoupled land systems by integrating value chain analysis with satellite-based degradation mapping. The findings will critically inform the design of 'deforestation-free' supply chain policies and international accountability mechanisms for reforestation pledges.
proposals.novelty: While leakage is studied in REDD+ projects, its systematic analysis at the national policy scale, integrating trade data with advanced remote sensing for degradation, is novel. It moves beyond a simple forest-cover metric to assess the full land-system sustainability of reforestation.

proposals.title: Spatial Optimization of Trade Flows to Reduce Deforestation Risk in Agricultural Commodity Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: International trade in agricultural commodities is a primary driver of tropical deforestation, yet trade policies and corporate zero-deforestation commitments often lack spatial specificity, failing to account for the heterogeneous biodiversity impacts of sourcing from different regions. Pre-2026 research, including Lambin's work, shows that value chain factors critically influence the adoption of sustainable practices, but these insights are rarely integrated with spatial conservation planning. This gap limits the effectiveness of policies in steering trade away from high-conservation-value, high-deforestation-risk landscapes.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating spatial conservation prioritization algorithms with trade flow models can identify optimal, biodiversity-sensitive sourcing patterns that significantly reduce the embedded deforestation risk in commodity supply chains compared to current, non-spatial policy approaches.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution land-use change data (e.g., Hansen/GFC) and species distribution models (IUCN) to create a spatial layer of biodiversity vulnerability to agricultural expansion. Using the Zonation conservation planning algorithm, we will prioritize landscapes where trade-linked deforestation imposes the highest biodiversity cost. This spatial cost layer will be integrated into a multi-regional input-output (MRIO) trade model (e.g., EXIOBASE) to simulate and optimize global commodity flows, minimizing total biodiversity impact under constraints of market demand and production feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, spatially explicit framework to assess and design trade policies, moving beyond binary 'deforestation-free' claims to quantify and minimize biodiversity footprints. It will directly inform the implementation of the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) and corporate due diligence by identifying high-priority sourcing regions and feasible alternative trade corridors.
proposals.novelty: While conservation planning and trade analysis are mature fields, their integration to actively optimize trade policy for biodiversity outcomes is novel. This approach moves from mapping the problem to generating spatially intelligent solutions, directly linking Lambin's work on value chain factors with actionable, algorithm-driven policy design.

proposals.title: Modeling a Closed-Loop Global System: A Theoretical Framework for Forest Transitions Without International Leakage
proposals.motivation: Despite progress in deforestation-free supply chain commitments, evidence suggests that reduced deforestation in one region often displaces land-use change and associated environmental impacts to other countries, undermining global forest conservation goals. This international leakage, or 'forest footprint displacement', is a critical unresolved challenge for global sustainability governance. A theoretical model of a closed global system is needed to understand the necessary conditions for achieving genuine zero-net deforestation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a global forest transition without international displacement is theoretically achievable only under a specific, integrated set of conditions governing commodity production, trade, and consumption, which can be formally modeled as a closed-loop system.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-region, multi-commodity system dynamics model representing global land systems as a closed mass-balance system. The model will integrate pre-2026 data on agricultural yields, trade flows, and consumption patterns. Key algorithms will include material flow analysis to track embodied deforestation in traded commodities and agent-based modeling components to simulate policy interventions (e.g., demand-side reductions, sustainable intensification, supply chain transparency) and their feedbacks, ensuring zero net displacement of land-use change across system boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal theoretical framework defining the necessary and sufficient conditions for a leakage-free global forest transition. It will advance the field of land system science by moving beyond case studies of displacement to a systemic, testable model. The findings will offer concrete, integrated policy targets for synchronizing production and consumption policies across jurisdictions.
proposals.novelty: While leakage is widely acknowledged, existing models are diagnostic; this proposal is prescriptive, constructing a theoretical 'closed world' to derive the principles for its achievement. It uniquely combines Lambin's expertise in value chain factors with a rigorous physical-closure constraint from systems engineering, a novel synthesis in land-use science.

proposals.title: Testing the Dominant Driver Hypothesis: A Causal Inference and Deep Learning Analysis of Deforestation in Commodity Frontiers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 land system science strongly emphasizes the synergy of multiple drivers (e.g., agricultural expansion, logging, infrastructure) in causing land-use change. This paradigm, while robust, may obscure instances where a single, powerful factor—such as a specific commodity value chain shock—is sufficient to trigger widespread deforestation. Understanding if and when such dominant drivers exist is critical for designing more targeted and effective conservation policies and supply chain interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: In specific commodity frontiers, a single, dominant factor (e.g., a price surge, a new road segment, or a policy shift within a supply chain) can be the primary and sufficient cause of deforestation, with other factors playing negligible or contingent roles.
proposals.method: We will combine causal inference techniques with high-resolution satellite imagery analysis. For selected cocoa and oil palm frontiers, we will construct panel datasets of deforestation (using Landsat/Sentinel-2) and candidate drivers (prices, road networks, governance). We will apply a Double Machine Learning (DML) framework to isolate the causal effect of a hypothesized dominant driver, controlling for confounders. Concurrently, a time-series deep learning model (e.g., a ConvLSTM) will be trained to segment and classify land-cover change sequences, with model interpretation techniques (e.g., SHAP) used to identify the primary signal in the pixel-level data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical test of the dominant driver hypothesis, challenging the prevailing necessity of synergy in land-change theory. It will offer a novel methodological framework integrating causal inference with remote sensing for driver attribution. The findings will directly inform the design of 'deforestation-free' supply chain policies by identifying the most critical leverage points for intervention.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly challenges the foundational 'synergy' paradigm in land system science. Methodologically, it moves beyond correlation by applying cutting-edge (pre-2026) causal inference techniques (DML) to remote sensing data, a combination not yet standard in the field, to test for sufficiency rather than just association.

proposals.title: Testing the Universality of Land-Use Transition Theory: A Complex Systems Analysis of Forest Cover Dynamics in Temperate and Boreal Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: The complex systems framework for analyzing land-use change, developed primarily in tropical deforestation contexts, posits interactions between distant drivers, local factors, and feedback loops. Its applicability beyond the tropics remains largely untested. Temperate and boreal regions face distinct pressures—from intensive forestry to climate-induced disturbances—and are embedded in different global commodity networks, making them critical testbeds for the theory's universality.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex systems framework of land-use transitions, integrating telecoupled supply chain drivers with local socio-ecological factors, is generalizable to temperate and boreal forests, but the dominant feedback mechanisms and the role of policy will differ systematically from tropical patterns.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-temporal land-cover maps (e.g., from Landsat and Sentinel-2) processed via a U-Net deep learning architecture for precise forest loss/gain segmentation with spatially explicit data on commodity flows (e.g., timber, pulp, agricultural products). Using panel regression and agent-based modeling, we will analyze causal pathways linking specific value chain actors (e.g., corporate pledges, certification schemes) to on-the-ground outcomes in selected North American and European regions. The analysis will explicitly model feedbacks, such as conservation policies triggered by perceived loss.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first systematic test of tropical land-use transition theory in temperate/boreal systems, potentially refining it into a universally applicable framework. It will generate novel insights into how sustainable supply chain policies function in industrialized forestry and agriculture contexts, informing more targeted governance.
proposals.novelty: Shifts the analytical focus of a dominant socio-ecological framework from the Global South to the Global North, explicitly coupling advanced remote sensing of non-tropical forests with value chain analysis designed for complex, corporate-driven commodity networks.

proposals.title: Climate as a Direct Driver: Quantifying the Causal Pathways from Climate Stress to Deforestation in Agricultural Commodity Frontiers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on land-use change predominantly treats climate as a contextual modifier of socio-economic drivers. However, increasing climate variability and extreme events (e.g., droughts, shifting precipitation patterns) are directly altering agricultural productivity and farmer livelihoods. This project addresses a critical gap by investigating whether and how climate anomalies act as a primary, direct driver of deforestation decisions, particularly within globally traded commodity supply chains, which is essential for designing resilient land-use policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific climate stressors (e.g., drought intensity, heatwaves) directly trigger deforestation events by degrading farm-level productivity and economic viability, and that this causal effect is mediated and amplified by value-chain structures (e.g., contract flexibility, buyer power) and existing policy frameworks.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution climate data (CHIRPS, ERA5) with near-real-time deforestation alerts (e.g., GLAD, RADD) and supply chain mapping data for key commodities (cocoa, soy) in frontier regions. Using a panel data structure, we will apply a Causal Forest machine learning algorithm to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects of climate stressors on deforestation probability. A structural equation model will then quantify the mediating roles of value-chain factors (from farmer surveys) and conservation policies.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first systematic, causal evidence quantifying climate as a direct driver of deforestation within specific commodity systems. It will advance land system science by formally integrating biophysical drivers into causal models of land-use change and offer practical insights for designing 'climate-smart' supply chain interventions and adaptation policies that preempt climate-induced forest loss.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the causal inference framework that positions climate not as a background covariate but as a primary treatment variable, and in its explicit integration with Lambin's established work on value-chain governance to uncover context-specific mediation pathways.

proposals.title: Millennial Legacies in Modern Land Systems: Integrating Paleoecology and Archaeology to Quantify Path Dependence in Tropical Forest Transitions
proposals.motivation: Contemporary land-use transitions are often analyzed within recent socio-economic or policy contexts, neglecting the profound, long-term legacies of past human-environment interactions. Pre-2026 research increasingly recognizes that current deforestation frontiers and agricultural systems may be shaped by millennial-scale legacies, but a robust, quantitative framework linking deep-time data to modern land-change science is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of path dependence and the true baseline conditions for sustainable land management.
proposals.hypothesis: Modern patterns of forest resilience, agricultural suitability, and land-use intensity in tropical regions are significantly influenced by the nature, timing, and spatial configuration of pre-colonial and early historical land-use transitions, creating measurable path dependencies that persist over millennia.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution paleoecological (pollen, charcoal) and archaeological (settlement, artifact) datasets for selected tropical regions (e.g., West Africa, Amazonia). Using a temporal convolutional network (TCN) architecture, we will model sequences of land-cover transitions derived from these proxies over the last 3000 years. This deep learning approach is suited for long sequential data. We will then statistically correlate these historical transition pathways with contemporary satellite-derived metrics of forest fragmentation, biomass, and land-use intensity from the Landsat and Sentinel archives, using spatial regression models to control for recent drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will pioneer a methodological bridge between paleoscience and contemporary land system science, providing a quantitative test of millennial-scale path dependence. It will generate novel maps of 'legacy susceptibility' to inform conservation and restoration planning by identifying areas where ancient disturbances may still influence ecosystem recovery. It will extend Lambin's transitions framework to a truly long-term ecological perspective.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal, data-driven integration of millennial-scale archaeological and paleoecological sequences with modern remote sensing through deep learning sequence models, moving beyond narrative analogies to test specific hypotheses about legacy effects on contemporary land systems.

proposals.title: The Resilience Paradox: How Import-Dependent Food Supply Chains Undermine Local Agricultural Sustainability in the Tropics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, global sustainability efforts often assume that importing food commodities can spare land and reduce deforestation in producer countries (the 'land sparing' logic). However, this may create a dangerous dependency, eroding local agricultural knowledge, infrastructure, and adaptive capacity. This research directly tests whether heavy reliance on food imports, often a result of specializing in export commodity production, makes local food systems more vulnerable to global shocks and less capable of adopting sustainable practices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in tropical agricultural landscapes, a high degree of reliance on imported staple foods is negatively correlated with indicators of local agricultural resilience and the adoption of sustainable farming practices, after controlling for economic and biophysical factors.
proposals.method: We will combine multi-sensor satellite imagery (Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2, Landsat) with national trade databases (FAOSTAT) and farmer survey data. A deep learning model (U-Net architecture) will be trained to segment croplands and classify farming practices (e.g., agroforestry, soil conservation) at a high resolution. We will construct a panel dataset at the sub-national level, linking import dependency ratios to metrics of agricultural resilience (crop diversity, landscape connectivity) and practice adoption. Causal pathways will be analyzed using structural equation modeling, integrating value chain factors from farmer surveys to explain the mechanisms behind observed correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence challenging the simplistic narrative that imports unilaterally aid preservation, reframing the debate on sustainable food systems to include resilience and local capacity. It will advance the integration of value chain analysis with spatially explicit land-use science, offering policymakers a diagnostic tool to identify regions at risk of 'resilience leakage' due to trade patterns.
proposals.novelty: While Lambin's prior work examines how value chains influence sustainable practices *for export commodities*, this proposal inverts the focus to examine how import dependency for *local consumption* affects systemic resilience—a link not explicitly made in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely merges granular trade data with deep learning-derived land-use practice maps to test this systemic relationship.

proposals.title: Sub-national and transnational drivers of land-use transitions: A multi-scale analysis of deforestation and agricultural intensification
proposals.motivation: National-level analyses of land-use change often mask significant sub-national heterogeneity and obscure the influence of transnational actors and supply chains. Pre-2026 research, including Lambin's work, shows that commodity-driven deforestation is shaped by local governance, corporate sourcing policies, and cross-border investment networks. A focus on the nation-state as the primary unit of analysis limits our understanding of these multi-scale drivers and the design of effective, targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Land-use transitions, particularly deforestation and the adoption of sustainable agricultural practices, are more strongly associated with the economic and policy characteristics of sub-national administrative units (e.g., states, provinces) and the structure of transnational commodity networks than with aggregate country-level factors.
proposals.method: We will integrate spatially explicit data on land-cover change (e.g., from Landsat, Sentinel-2) with sub-national socio-economic datasets and a novel database of transnational corporate linkages and sourcing regions for key commodities (soy, cocoa, palm oil). Using a hierarchical modeling framework, we will quantify the relative influence of sub-national (e.g., local governance, infrastructure) and transnational (e.g., buyer commitments, trade agreements) factors. Deep learning segmentation (U-Net architectures) will be applied to high-resolution imagery to map fine-scale agricultural practices and forest loss, linking these patterns to specific value chain actors identified via corporate disclosures and traceability data.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, multi-scale empirical framework for analyzing land-system change, moving beyond country-level correlations. It will directly inform the implementation of sub-national jurisdictional approaches to deforestation and the evaluation of corporate zero-deforestation commitments by pinpointing the geographic and governance scales where interventions are most effective.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines either local drivers or global trade, this study innovatively models them simultaneously within a unified analytical framework, explicitly testing the relative explanatory power of sub-national and transnational units against national-level factors.

proposals.title: Mapping the Embedded Deforestation in Global Commodity Networks: A Multi-Regional Input-Output and Remote Sensing Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Despite proliferating zero-deforestation commitments by corporations, the complex, multi-tiered nature of global supply chains obscures the true origin and land-use impacts of traded commodities. Pre-2026 research highlights the critical role of value chain factors in enabling or hindering sustainable practices, but a systematic, high-resolution method to link specific deforestation frontiers to final consumption via intermediate trade nodes is lacking. This gap impedes effective policy targeting and accountability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of deforestation embodied in agricultural commodities is mediated through a small number of intermediary trade hubs and processing countries, which dilute and obscure the direct linkage between frontier deforestation and final consumer markets.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-regional input-output (MRIO) models (e.g., EXIOBASE) with high-resolution, temporally matched remote sensing data (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2) and customs trade data. The MRIO analysis will trace embodied land-use change and commodity flows. We will employ a network analysis framework, using algorithms for community detection (e.g., Louvain method) and centrality metrics (e.g., betweenness, eigenvector centrality) to identify critical nodes and pathways. This quantitative network will be validated and enriched with qualitative data from key informant interviews in identified hub regions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a spatially explicit map of deforestation risk transmission through global supply chains, moving beyond bilateral trade statistics. It will contribute a novel methodological framework that tightly couples physical land-change science with economic network analysis. The findings will offer evidence-based insights for designing more effective supply chain interventions and international trade policies that target leverage points.
proposals.novelty: While MRIO and remote sensing are established, their integration to dynamically link *specific, sub-national* deforestation events to *entire global trade networks* represents a significant advance. The novelty lies in applying network theory from industrial ecology to diagnose the structural opacity, not just the volume, of embedded deforestation.

proposals.title: Modeling the Resilience of Deforestation-Free Supply Chains: An Agent-Based Analysis of Local Land-Use Decisions under Global Market Volatility
proposals.motivation: Despite proliferating zero-deforestation commitments, the resilience of sustainable commodity supply chains to global economic shocks remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 evidence shows that local land-use decisions are influenced by complex interactions between value chain factors and macro-economic signals, yet existing models often fail to capture these cross-scale feedbacks, limiting effective policy design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the resilience of deforestation-free supply chains to price volatility is non-linear and depends critically on the heterogeneity of farmer agents and the structure of value chain intermediaries, with tipping points leading to rapid reversion to deforestation.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit agent-based model (ABM) using a framework like NetLogo or Mesa. The model will integrate heterogeneous farmer agents (differentiated by size, capital, and risk aversion) who make land-use decisions based on behavioral rules derived from Lambin's prior survey work. These agents will be embedded in a multi-tier value chain network (traders, cooperatives, exporters) that transmits global commodity price signals and sustainability premiums. We will calibrate and validate the model using panel data from cocoa regions in Ecuador/Uganda and satellite-derived land-cover change maps (leveraging pre-2026 deep learning segmentation techniques). Simulation experiments will test policy interventions under varying price shock scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, mechanistic understanding of how cross-scale interactions determine the stability of sustainable land systems. It will offer policymakers and companies a simulation tool to stress-test supply chain interventions and identify leverage points that enhance resilience against market shocks.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs exist for land-use, few integrate detailed, empirically grounded value chain architectures with global economic signals to explicitly test the resilience of deforestation-free commitments, bridging Lambin's work on supply chains with complexity science.

proposals.title: Necessary and Sufficient Policy Mixes for Forest Transition: A Qualitative Comparative Analysis of Subnational Jurisdictions
proposals.motivation: Achieving forest transitions—shifts from net deforestation to net reforestation—is a critical sustainability goal, yet the specific combinations of policies that enable them remain poorly understood. Single-policy analyses are insufficient, as real-world outcomes result from complex interactions between regulations, incentives, and value chain interventions. A systematic, comparative method is needed to identify the policy configurations that are consistently effective across diverse socio-ecological contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that no single policy is sufficient to induce a forest transition, but that specific, identifiable combinations of public regulations, market-based incentives, and supply-chain governance measures are both necessary and sufficient for sustained transitions in commodity-producing regions.
proposals.method: We will employ crisp-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (csQCA), a method from political science suited for medium-N comparative case studies. We will construct a dataset of 30-40 subnational jurisdictions (e.g., provinces, states) in tropical commodity frontiers that have exhibited either a forest transition or continued deforestation over the 2005-2020 period. Using pre-2026 satellite-derived land-cover data (e.g., Hansen/GFC) and policy databases, we will code binary conditions for the presence/absence of key policy types: strict land-use zoning, payments for ecosystem services, deforestation-free supply chain commitments, and agricultural intensification support. The QCA algorithm will then identify the minimal combinations of conditions (policy mixes) associated with the outcome (forest transition).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a typology of effective policy mixes for land-use sustainability, moving beyond generic recommendations to context-specific recipes. It will directly inform the design of coherent policy bundles for jurisdictions engaged in zero-deforestation agreements and contribute to the theoretical understanding of multi-level governance in land systems.
proposals.novelty: While QCA is established in political science, its application to diagnose effective policy combinations for socio-ecological transitions like forest recovery is novel. It integrates Lambin's expertise on land-use change drivers and value chains with a formal method for analyzing causal complexity, bridging a gap between land system science and policy mix theory.

proposals.title: Optimizing Land Zoning for Forest Ecosystem Services Under Food Security Constraints: A Value Chain-Informed Spatial Planning Framework
proposals.motivation: Global efforts to halt deforestation, such as zero-deforestation supply chain commitments, often conflict with the imperative to increase food production. This creates a critical land-use dilemma: how to meet fixed regional or national food needs while maximizing the forest ecosystem services (e.g., carbon sequestration, biodiversity, hydrological regulation) essential for climate resilience and human well-being. Pre-2026 research has largely treated these objectives separately or focused on minimizing deforestation for a given production level, not explicitly maximizing services given a food constraint.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating spatially explicit data on agricultural value chain drivers with biophysical models of ecosystem service provision will reveal non-obvious, Pareto-superior land zoning configurations that can meet a fixed food production target while significantly enhancing forest service provision compared to business-as-usual or protection-only scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit optimization model using a multi-objective genetic algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) to solve the dual problem. The model will integrate: 1) high-resolution land cover maps and ecosystem service models (e.g., InVEST for carbon, sediment retention) derived from satellite imagery (Landsat, Sentinel-2); 2) a food production module calibrated with crop yields and constrained by a fixed calorie or protein target; and 3) a value chain factor layer mapping accessibility to markets, contracts, and infrastructure influencing farming practices, based on Lambin's prior work. The algorithm will iteratively evaluate zoning plans (protected, sustainable intensification, conventional agriculture) to identify optimal trade-off frontiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel analytical framework to move beyond binary forest-agriculture trade-offs, offering policymakers and commodity chain actors spatially explicit pathways to align food security with ecosystem service goals. It will advance land system science by formally integrating value chain sociology with biophysical optimization, and deliver a replicable method to assess the feasibility of deforestation-free commitments under production constraints.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovates by framing the core challenge as a service-maximization (not just loss-minimization) problem under a hard food constraint and by explicitly incorporating the socio-economic drivers of land use from value chain analysis into a spatial optimization algorithm, a synthesis not yet prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Decoupling Forest Preservation from Food Import Dependence: A Value Chain and Land-Use Efficiency Analysis
proposals.motivation: Policies to curb deforestation, such as zero-deforestation supply chain commitments, risk displacing agricultural production and increasing reliance on imported food, potentially exporting environmental impacts. This trade-off challenges the sustainability and equity of forest conservation efforts. It is critical to empirically test whether territorial forest preservation can be achieved while maintaining or enhancing local food self-sufficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Forest preservation at a sub-national or national scale can be achieved without increasing net food imports by improving land-use efficiency and reconfiguring domestic agricultural value chains to reduce pressure on forest frontiers.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution land-cover change maps (e.g., from Sentinel-2) derived via a U-Net deep learning architecture with sub-national agricultural statistics and trade data for selected tropical commodity frontiers (e.g., cocoa in West Africa, soy in South America). A panel regression analysis will model forest loss/gain against indicators of agricultural productivity, value chain governance (e.g., certification, buyer contracts), and food trade balances, controlling for other factors. Counterfactual scenarios will assess the impact of sustainable intensification and shortened supply chains.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, spatially explicit test of the forest-food import nexus, moving beyond theoretical trade-off models. It will generate evidence for policymakers on how value chain interventions and productivity gains can simultaneously support forest and food security goals, contributing to more place-based sustainable development strategies.
proposals.novelty: While studies analyze deforestation drivers or sustainable value chains separately, this work novelly integrates these lenses to directly test the causal assumption that forest preservation necessitates import dependency, using advanced remote sensing to link land outcomes to specific chain configurations.

proposals.title: Beyond Global Markets: Quantifying the Dominance of Domestic Drivers in Forest Conversion Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Globalization and commodity trade are widely cited as primary amplifiers of land-use change, yet this focus may overshadow significant domestic drivers. Understanding the relative weight of domestic factors (e.g., local governance, infrastructure, demographic shifts, national policies) versus international supply chains is critical for designing effective, context-specific land-use policies. This research addresses a key gap in land system science by systematically testing the assumed primacy of globalization across different conversion mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: For at least two of the four primary land conversion mechanisms (e.g., agricultural expansion, timber extraction, urbanization, mining), domestic socio-economic and political factors are the dominant drivers, exerting a stronger influence than international market integration in a majority of cases studied.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale, mixed-methods design. First, a spatially explicit panel analysis will use high-resolution (e.g., Sentinel-2) land-cover change data (2015-2025) derived via a U-Net deep learning architecture for segmentation.
