This will be linked to georeferenced datasets on domestic factors (road networks, agricultural subsidies, population density) and global trade exposure (proximity to ports, commodity prices). A multi-level regression model will partition variance. Second, qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) of selected value chains (e.g., cocoa, palm oil) in contrasting countries will trace how domestic policy and local market structures filter global demand.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a robust, empirical quantification of the relative importance of domestic versus global drivers, moving beyond case-study anecdotes. It will generate a novel diagnostic framework to classify regions where land-use interventions should target local governance versus international supply chain governance. The findings will directly inform the debate on the efficacy of deforestation-free supply chain policies versus domestic land-use zoning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Lambin and others has analyzed value chain factors, this proposal explicitly tests their primacy against a comprehensive suite of domestic drivers across multiple conversion mechanisms. The integration of deep learning-derived land-change data with multi-level modeling and QCA offers a novel methodological synthesis to disentangle scalar causality in land systems.

proposals.title: Leveraging Value Chain Governance and Satellite Monitoring to Design Policies for Land-Use Efficient Global Agricultural Trade
proposals.motivation: Globalization drives agricultural expansion into forests, as capital and trade demand often prioritize short-term yield increases over sustainable intensification. Existing zero-deforestation commitments are fragmented and rarely incentivize systemic shifts towards using already-cleared land more efficiently. A policy framework is needed to redirect global financial and market forces to actively reduce the land footprint of commodity production.
proposals.hypothesis: Policies that explicitly link preferential market access and finance to verifiable, landscape-scale improvements in land-use efficiency—rather than just the absence of deforestation—can decouple agricultural production from frontier expansion in globalized commodity chains.
proposals.method: We will integrate value chain analysis with remote sensing. First, we will map governance structures and financial flows in soy and cocoa chains using stakeholder surveys and document analysis. Concurrently, using Sentinel-2 and Landsat 8 imagery, we will train a U-Net deep learning architecture to classify crop types and estimate yield proxies, enabling the calculation of production-to-land-use-change ratios at the municipal level over time (2015-2025). These geospatial efficiency metrics will be statistically linked to chain-specific policy variables (e.g., contract terms, credit sources) to identify leverage points.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, evidence-based policy toolkit for governments and multi-national corporations to structure trade and investment contracts that reward sustainable intensification. It will advance the scientific integration of value chain economics with spatially explicit land-use science, moving beyond deforestation-free to 'land-smart' supply chain governance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on halting deforestation, this study innovates by directly targeting the redirection of global capital and trade to actively improve land-use efficiency as a measurable outcome, using integrated socio-economic and deep learning-derived biophysical data.

proposals.title: Modeling the Resilience of Deforestation-Free Supply Chains: An Agent-Based Framework of Landowner Decisions Under Global Capital Flows
proposals.motivation: The effectiveness of zero-deforestation commitments in agricultural supply chains remains uncertain, as local land-use decisions are increasingly influenced by distant market signals and global capital flows. Pre-2026 research highlights a critical gap in understanding how these global economic forces interact with local socio-ecological contexts to either reinforce or undermine sustainable land-use transitions. A modeling framework that integrates these multi-scale feedbacks is essential for designing policies that are robust to economic volatility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the resilience of deforestation-free supply chains to commodity price shocks is non-linearly dependent on the heterogeneity of local landowner agents and the structure of capital flows from downstream value chain actors.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit agent-based model (ABM) where individual landowner agents make land-use decisions based on pre-2026 behavioral theories (e.g., Theory of Planned Behavior) and locally perceived constraints. These agents will be embedded within a network model of global capital flows, simulating investments and price premiums from traders, processors, and retailers committed to sustainable sourcing. The model will be parameterized and validated using existing data on cocoa and palm oil value chains, with land-cover change outputs compared against high-resolution satellite imagery analyzed via convolutional neural networks (CNNs), a technique demonstrated in Lambin's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically grounded computational laboratory to test the interaction of value chain policies and local decision-making. It will advance the field of land system science by providing a formal framework to quantify the conditions under which global sustainability signals translate to effective local action, moving beyond correlational studies. The findings will offer actionable insights for policymakers and corporations on leveraging capital flows to stabilize sustainable transitions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit coupling of a micro-founded agent-based model of land-use with a macro-scale model of financial flows within commodity networks, a synthesis not yet achieved in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond static chain-of-custody models to simulate dynamic, two-way feedbacks between global capital and local landscapes.

proposals.title: Mapping Telecoupled Land-Use Change: A Network Analysis of Trade, Remittances, and Foreign Direct Investment
proposals.motivation: Global land-use change is increasingly driven by distant socio-economic actors through commodity trade, financial flows, and remittances, creating complex telecouplings. Pre-2026 research on deforestation drivers often focuses on single commodities or bilateral trade, failing to capture the integrated, multi-flow nature of these globalized connections. A systemic understanding of how these financial and trade networks interact to influence land-use decisions is critical for designing effective, targeted sustainability policies.
proposals.hypothesis: The structure and centrality of countries within the multiplex global network of trade, foreign direct investment (FDI), and remittances significantly predict patterns of land-use change, particularly deforestation, in agricultural frontier regions, with network effects outweighing or modifying local factors.
proposals.method: We will construct a multiplex network model where nodes are countries and layers represent (1) agricultural commodity trade flows (FAOSTAT), (2) FDI stocks in agriculture and mining (OECD, fDi Markets), and (3) bilateral remittance flows (World Bank). We will apply community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) to identify clusters of tightly connected countries. Using panel regression models, we will test if network metrics (e.g., eigenvector centrality, inter-layer correlation) for commodity-exporting countries explain annual deforestation rates (from Hansen et al. or Global Forest Watch), controlling for national-level factors. The analysis will cover 2005-2020.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first integrated, quantitative map of the global land-use telecoupling network, moving beyond single-commodity supply chain analyses. It will identify key leverage points in the global economy where interventions in capital or trade flows could most effectively reduce deforestation. The findings will directly inform international policies on sustainable finance and due diligence regulations.
proposals.novelty: While network theory has been applied to trade, and Lambin's work has examined value chain factors, the integration of remittances and FDI with trade into a single multiplex network model to explain land-use change is novel. This approach captures the full spectrum of capital flows driving land-use decisions, a synthesis not yet attempted in the land system science literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Remittance Flows and Land-Use Micro-Dynamics: A Village-Scale Analysis of Household Decision-Making in Agricultural Frontiers
proposals.motivation: Remittances are a critical financial flow to rural households in developing countries, yet their impact on local land-use decisions remains poorly understood at the granular scale. This gap limits the ability of land-use science and sustainable supply chain governance to predict how rural economies and deforestation pressures evolve with migration. Understanding the household-level mechanisms is essential for designing policies that align rural development with forest conservation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that remittance income leads to heterogeneous changes in household land-use decisions, with the direction and magnitude of change mediated by the household's position within agricultural commodity value chains and its pre-existing asset endowment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, village-scale survey in agricultural frontiers of West Africa and Southeast Asia to collect detailed socio-economic, remittance, and land-use data from households. Survey data will be integrated with high-resolution land-cover maps (e.g., Sentinel-2) using a U-Net convolutional neural network for precise parcel-level classification of cropland and forest cover change over a 5-year period. A structural equation model will then be used to quantify the causal pathways linking remittance levels to land-use outcomes, controlling for value chain linkages (e.g., contract farming) and local context.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a mechanistic, household-level model of how global financial flows (remittances) translate into local land-use change, moving beyond coarse national-level correlations. It will directly inform the design of 'deforestation-free' supply chain interventions by identifying which households are likely to intensify, abandon, or expand farmland with increased capital.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating fine-scale remote sensing of land use with detailed household surveys and value chain analysis to unpack the 'black box' of remittance impacts, moving from association to mechanism at the critical village scale where decisions aggregate to landscape patterns.

proposals.title: Optimizing Global Cropland Allocation to Minimize Agricultural Expansion While Meeting Caloric Demand
proposals.motivation: Global food demand drives agricultural expansion, a primary cause of deforestation and biodiversity loss. Pre-2026 research on sustainable supply chains and land-use change highlights the need for systemic solutions that decouple production from land conversion. This proposal addresses the critical gap between sector-specific interventions and a globally optimal land-use configuration for food security.
proposals.hypothesis: A global spatial optimization model, constrained by realistic agronomic, economic, and supply chain factors, can identify a cropland configuration that meets current global caloric output with significantly less total area than the existing pattern, primarily by shifting production to highest-yielding suitable regions.
proposals.method: We will develop a mixed-integer linear programming model to minimize global cropland area. The model will be constrained by pre-2026 global data on crop-specific yields (from SPAM), calorie content, and agro-ecological suitability. To ensure realism, we will incorporate key value chain factors (e.g., processing location, trade costs) as soft constraints. The optimization output—a theoretical 'minimum land map'—will be compared against 2020 cropland extent from ESA CCI Land Cover, with discrepancies analyzed to identify regions of potential land sparing and intensification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, spatially explicit benchmark for evaluating the efficiency of current global cropland use. It will quantify the potential land sparing achievable through optimized allocation, informing high-level policy on trade, storage, and agricultural development. The analysis will directly bridge Lambin's work on supply chain factors with macro-scale land-use planning.
proposals.novelty: While yield gap analyses are common, this approach uniquely frames the problem as a global spatial optimization with integrated value chain constraints, moving beyond biophysical potential to identify feasible, efficient land-use configurations. It applies operations research techniques typically used in logistics to the systemic problem of land-use efficiency.

proposals.title: Land Systems as Dissipative Structures: A Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamics Framework to Model Socio-Ecological Fluxes and Regime Stability
proposals.motivation: Dominant land system models often assume equilibrium or steady-state conditions, failing to capture the inherent dynamism and continuous change driven by coupled human-natural processes. This limits our ability to predict non-linear transitions, such as sudden deforestation or agricultural intensification, which are critical for sustainability governance. A theoretical framework grounded in the physics of open systems far from equilibrium could fundamentally improve the diagnosis and projection of land system change.
proposals.hypothesis: Land systems can be conceptualized as dissipative structures, where specific dynamic regimes (e.g., agro-industrial corridors, sustainable forest communities) are maintained by characteristic magnitudes and configurations of socio-ecological material and informational fluxes. Regime shifts occur when these sustaining fluxes are perturbed beyond critical thresholds, leading to a reorganized flux structure.
proposals.method: We will integrate spatially explicit material flow analysis (for biomass, nutrients, water) with value chain mapping (for capital, commodities, information) to quantify key fluxes for case studies of cocoa and palm oil systems. These flux networks will serve as input to develop an agent-based model (ABM) where land manager agents (farmers, corporations) interact based on flux availability and cost. We will adapt entropy production metrics from non-equilibrium thermodynamics to measure system disorder from flux data and use convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on satellite time-series (Landsat, Sentinel-2) to classify and validate the spatiotemporal stability of the modeled flux regimes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, process-based theoretical lens for land system science, moving beyond correlative land cover change analysis. It will yield a scalable methodological toolkit to quantify the 'metabolism' of land systems and diagnose pre-transition flux imbalances, offering actionable insights for designing interventions within commodity supply chains to enhance resilience.
proposals.novelty: While socio-metabolic studies exist, the explicit application of non-equilibrium thermodynamics concepts (dissipative structures, entropy production) to coupled socio-ecological land systems is novel. The integration of deep learning for remote sensing of flux-sustained patterns with agent-based modeling of flux-driven decisions creates a unique mixed-methods approach.

proposals.title: Inverting Land Cover Trajectories to Diagnose Socio-Ecological Regimes in Commodity-Producing Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Current models of land-use change often treat socio-economic factors as external drivers, creating a disconnect between observed land-cover patterns and the internal state of the coupled human-environment system. This limits our ability to diagnose the underlying governance, behavioral, and ecological conditions that determine a system's resilience and trajectory, which is critical for evaluating the real-world efficacy of sustainability policies and zero-deforestation commitments in commodity supply chains.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-series sequence of land-cover patterns observed from satellites contains sufficient information to probabilistically infer the latent internal state of a socio-ecological system, including key parameters of land manager behavior, institutional rule adherence, and ecological legacy effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep learning-based inverse modeling framework. A convolutional LSTM network will encode multi-temporal satellite imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2) into a latent state vector. This vector will be conditioned by, and must reconstruct, outputs from a forward process-based model (e.g., a modified agent-based land-use model) that simulates landscape change based on internal rules and states. Training will use contrastive learning to ensure the inferred latent states are discriminative of known socio-ecological regimes (e.g., certified vs. non-certified supply chain contexts) identified in pre-2026 fieldwork in cocoa and palm oil landscapes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool to map the de facto socio-ecological regime of a landscape from remote sensing alone, moving beyond correlation to system state estimation. It will directly contribute to monitoring the embedded human factors within sustainability transitions, offering a more nuanced assessment of commodity supply chain governance and resilience than current compliance-focused methods.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using socio-economic data to predict land cover, we invert the problem to infer the socio-ecological state from land-cover patterns, formally embedding human actors as stateful system components. This integrates deep learning's pattern recognition strength with the mechanistic understanding of process-based models in a novel inverse scheme.

proposals.title: From Land Cover to Land Function: Developing a Functional Trait Matrix for Modeling and Managing Ecosystem Service Provision
proposals.motivation: Current land system models and sustainability policies, including deforestation-free supply chain commitments, predominantly rely on categorical land cover classifications (e.g., forest, cropland). This approach is insufficient for predicting and managing the specific ecosystem services—like carbon sequestration, water regulation, and biodiversity habitat—that are critical for climate mitigation and sustainable livelihoods. A paradigm shift is needed to directly model the functional state of landscapes, enabling policies that target service outcomes rather than just cover labels.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-dimensional Functional Trait Matrix (FTM), derived from remote sensing and ecological data, can more accurately and directly represent ecosystem state and service provision than categorical land cover maps, thereby improving models of land-use change impacts on key environmental functions.
proposals.method: We will develop the FTM by selecting and quantifying key functional traits (e.g., canopy height/rugosity for structure, SIF/NDVI for productivity, land surface temperature for energy balance, spectral diversity for biodiversity proxies) using multi-source satellite data (Sentinel-1/2, Landsat, GEDI). A deep learning architecture, specifically a multi-task convolutional neural network (CNN), will be trained to simultaneously predict these continuous trait values from satellite imagery, building on methods used for aquatic vegetation segmentation. This FTM will then be integrated into an agent-based land-use change model to simulate how value chain factors and policies influence functional outcomes, not just cover transitions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, operational framework for monitoring and modeling landscapes based on their biophysical functions, directly linking remote sensing to ecosystem service metrics. It will offer a more nuanced evidence base for evaluating the environmental performance of commodity supply chains and the effectiveness of land-use policies aimed at specific functional outcomes like carbon storage or hydrological regulation.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit adoption of a materials science analogy—shifting from categorical types to functional properties—to reconceptualize the fundamental state variable in land system science. It moves beyond correlating cover with services to directly modeling services as the primary state.

proposals.title: Beyond Land Cover: Mapping Functional Ecosystem Services in Agricultural Landscapes to Assess Sustainability in Commodity Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: Current sustainability governance of agricultural supply chains (e.g., for cocoa, soy) often relies on binary deforestation metrics derived from land cover classification. This fails to capture the functional quality of landscapes, such as carbon storage, habitat connectivity, or hydrological regulation, which are critical for ecosystem resilience and long-term sustainability. A shift from monitoring land cover state to mapping ecosystem service flows is needed to evaluate the true environmental performance of sustainable farming practices and policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially explicit models of key ecosystem services (e.g., above-ground biomass, pollination potential, sediment retention) can be derived from satellite data and integrated with value chain information to reveal significant, policy-relevant mismatches between certified 'deforestation-free' supply chains and the actual functional integrity of the landscapes they source from.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-2026 deep learning architectures, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and U-Nets, trained on high-resolution satellite imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2) and ancillary data (e.g., LiDAR-derived canopy height, soil maps) to segment and map continuous variables representing ecosystem traits (e.g., vegetation structure, phenology). These traits will serve as inputs to established biophysical models (e.g., InVEST) to quantify ecosystem services. The resulting service maps will be statistically linked to farm-level data on value chain factors (e.g., certification, buyer contracts) and farming practices from field surveys in key commodity regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational framework for assessing agricultural sustainability based on functional landscape metrics rather than static land cover classes. It will directly inform policy and corporate due diligence by identifying which value chain factors are associated with the maintenance of multifunctional landscapes, moving beyond a simplistic focus on forest presence/absence.
proposals.novelty: While land cover mapping is mature, the integration of deep learning for direct trait estimation from satellites to model dynamic ecosystem services, and its explicit coupling with value chain analysis in sustainability science, represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance beyond Lambin's prior work on supply chain governance.

proposals.title: Coupling Land System Change and Climate Models: A Scale-Aware Emulator Framework to Quantify Uncertainty in Land-Climate Feedbacks
proposals.motivation: Projecting future climate requires integrating human-driven land-use change, a major source of uncertainty. However, a fundamental scale mismatch exists: land system models (LSMs) operate at fine, administrative scales capturing socio-economic drivers, while Earth System Models (ESMs) use coarse grids for physical processes. This disconnect impedes robust assessment of how land-use decisions, mediated by value chains and policies, feedback onto regional climate, limiting effective mitigation and adaptation planning.
proposals.hypothesis: A statistical land system emulator, trained on high-resolution LSM outputs and designed to preserve aggregate climate-relevant surface properties, can effectively bridge this scale gap. We hypothesize that coupling this emulator with an ESM via a probabilistic coupler will reveal how uncertainty in socio-economic projections propagates to uncertainty in climate outcomes, particularly for deforestation frontiers.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular framework with three core components. First, a 'land system emulator' built using a convolutional neural network (CNN) or a gradient-boosting model (e.g., XGBoost) trained on outputs from a high-resolution LSM (e.g., CLUMondo) to predict grid-cell level summaries of albedo, roughness length, and carbon stocks. Second, an 'uncertainty-quantifying coupler' will use Bayesian model averaging or an ensemble Kalman filter variant to translate these summaries into ESM boundary conditions, explicitly tracking uncertainty. Third, we will implement this framework in a targeted coupling with the Community Earth System Model (CESM) for a tropical deforestation hotspot, using pre-2026 remote sensing data (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2) for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source software tool for the land-climate modeling community, enabling more computationally efficient and uncertainty-aware exploration of feedbacks. It will advance the empirical understanding of how specific land-use transitions, influenced by commodity supply chain dynamics, modulate local and regional climate, offering actionable insights for designing 'climate-smart' land-use policies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has coupled models, our approach uniquely focuses on emulating the *process* of fine-scale land system change for climate modeling, not just static maps, and explicitly quantifies the propagation of uncertainty from human decision-making through the coupled system, a critical gap identified in Lambin's work on deforestation-free supply chains.

proposals.title: Functional Connectivity Archetypes of Land Systems: A Global Typology for Resilience Based on Ecological Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current global land system classifications rely heavily on static land cover proportions, failing to capture the functional connectivity that underpins resilience and ecosystem service provision. This gap limits our ability to predict how systems will respond to pressures like agricultural expansion or climate change, which is critical for designing effective sustainability policies and supply chain interventions. A network-based typology can diagnose systemic vulnerabilities and identify regions at risk of crossing ecological or socio-economic tipping points.
proposals.hypothesis: Land systems can be classified into globally recurrent archetypes based on the structural and functional properties of their internal ecological and socio-economic networks, and these network archetypes are stronger predictors of system resilience and response to change than traditional land cover composition metrics.
proposals.method: We will first compile a global database of dense local case studies (e.g., from Lambin's work on cocoa and deforestation frontiers) and characterize each as a spatial network of land cover patches. Using graph theory, we will compute metrics like connectivity, modularity, and centrality for flows of species, nutrients, and capital derived from remote sensing (e.g., Sentinel-2), fieldwork, and economic data. These case-level network metrics will be upscaled using a machine learning pipeline: a convolutional neural network (CNN) will extract network-relevant features from global satellite imagery time-series, which will then be linked to the case metrics via a transfer learning framework. Finally, global archetypes will be generated by clustering these predicted functional metrics using an unsupervised algorithm like hierarchical clustering or a Gaussian mixture model.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first globally consistent diagnostic map of land systems based on functional network properties, directly linking local case-study depth to global scalability. It will provide a novel framework for assessing the resilience of commodity-producing regions, informing the design of more spatially targeted 'deforestation-free' supply chain policies. The typology will offer a predictive tool for identifying regions most susceptible to regime shifts under land-use change.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond correlating land cover with outcomes by explicitly modeling the internal network structure of land systems as the basis for a global classification. It fuses Lambin's expertise in local land-change processes and value chain analysis with cutting-edge pre-2026 deep learning and ecological network methods to upscale functional understanding.

proposals.title: Mapping the Resilience Frontier: A Participatory Multi-Objective Control Framework for Navigating Trade-offs in Agricultural Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Land systems face intensifying pressures to deliver multiple, often competing, resilience objectives such as food security, biodiversity conservation, and climate mitigation. Pre-2026 research, including on deforestation-free supply chains, shows that top-down policies often fail because they ignore local stakeholder values and the inherent trade-offs between these objectives. This creates conflict and undermines the legitimacy and effectiveness of sustainability interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: A control-theoretic model, co-developed with stakeholders to quantify their resilience objectives, can map the Pareto-optimal frontier of land management strategies, thereby making explicit and negotiable the trade-offs that are typically hidden in conventional planning.
proposals.method: We will first conduct participatory workshops with farmers, cooperatives, and policymakers in a commodity-producing region to co-define and quantify resilience objectives (e.g., yield stability, forest cover, income). These will inform a dynamic land system model, where management actions are control inputs. We will then employ multi-objective optimization algorithms, specifically the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II), to compute the Pareto frontier of strategies under different climate and market scenarios. The frontier will be integrated into an interactive visual decision-support tool for stakeholder negotiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework that bridges participatory stakeholder engagement with formal control and optimization theory for land system planning. It will advance the field of sustainability science by offering a concrete tool to operationalize the concept of 'resilience trade-offs,' moving beyond qualitative discussions to quantifiable, negotiable scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While multi-objective optimization is known, its integration with participatory control-theoretic modeling of land systems—explicitly designed to visualize and negotiate value conflicts in resilience planning—is a novel synthesis of Lambin's work on value chains, stakeholder practices, and geospatial analysis.

proposals.title: Beyond the Regional Goldilocks Zone: A Multi-Scale Assessment of Monitoring Efficacy for Deforestation Drivers and Supply Chain Interventions
proposals.motivation: Land system science and sustainability governance have long operated under an implicit assumption that regional scales (e.g., biomes, jurisdictions) are optimal for monitoring socio-environmental change, balancing detail and generality. However, this assumption is untested for many specific processes. For actionable insights—particularly for verifying deforestation-free supply chains or targeting sustainable practice adoption—the most informative scale may be either hyper-local (farm/plantation) or global (commodity flows), depending on the driver and policy lever.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal spatial scale for monitoring land-use change to generate actionable insights is not universally regional but is contingent on the specific change process: global supply chain linkages are best monitored at a global scale, while the adoption of on-farm sustainable practices is best assessed at a hyper-local scale.
proposals.method: We will analyze deforestation and agricultural intensification in cocoa and oil palm systems using a multi-scale remote sensing and data fusion framework. A U-Net deep learning architecture, pre-trained on datasets like Sentinel-2, will perform fine-scale land cover segmentation to identify farm boundaries and practices. Concurrently, a global-scale analysis will use attention-based models (e.g., Transformer architectures) to link commodity trade data with aggregated deforestation signals from MODIS and VIIRS. The actionable insight from each scale will be evaluated against ground-truthed data on policy effectiveness and farmer behavior from existing surveys in Ecuador, Uganda, and Indonesia.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, empirical framework for selecting monitoring scales based on the specific policy or management question, moving beyond convention. It will directly inform the design of more effective monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) systems for corporate due diligence (e.g., EU Deforestation Regulation) and targeted agricultural extension programs.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale analysis is common, explicitly testing the 'actionable insight' yield of global versus hyper-local scales for specific socio-ecological processes—and integrating deep learning-based local segmentation with global trade-attention models—is a novel synthesis of Lambin's work on value chains and remote sensing.

proposals.title: Modeling Policy Feedback Loops: A Causal Inference Framework to Quantify How Ecosystem Degradation Forces Adaptation in Land-Use Governance
proposals.motivation: Current models of land-use change overwhelmingly treat policy as an exogenous driver, neglecting the critical feedback where environmental degradation itself triggers policy adaptation. This unidirectional view limits our understanding of socio-ecological system resilience and the effectiveness of governance. Grounded in pre-2026 knowledge of complex systems and policy feedback theory, this research addresses a core gap in explaining the dynamic co-evolution of ecosystems and institutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that measurable thresholds of ecosystem change (e.g., forest fragmentation, biodiversity loss) are statistically significant causal drivers of subsequent policy modifications, and that the strength and direction of this influence are mediated by specific value-chain and market factors.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-stage causal inference framework. First, using high-resolution satellite time-series (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2) and pre-2026 deep learning architectures (specifically, U-Net for consistent land-cover segmentation and LSTMs for change detection), we will quantify spatiotemporal patterns of deforestation and degradation. Second, we will construct a novel, granular policy database coding the timing, stringency, and geographic scope of land-use regulations. Finally, we will apply Structural Causal Models and Difference-in-Differences designs with instrumental variables to identify causal links from ecosystem state variables to policy outcomes, controlling for socio-economic confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically grounded framework for modeling bidirectional policy-ecosystem interactions, moving beyond static impact assessments. It will provide quantitative evidence on how environmental thresholds trigger governance shifts, offering critical insights for designing adaptive and anticipatory policies within commodity supply chains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the causal direction of standard land-use change models to treat policy as an endogenous variable forced by ecosystem state, and in formally testing this relationship using cutting-edge (pre-2026) causal inference techniques combined with remote sensing.

proposals.title: A Question-Driven Framework for Land-Cover Change Detection: Matching Remote Sensing Methods to Policy-Relevant Outcomes in Tropical Commodity Frontiers
proposals.motivation: Remote sensing for deforestation monitoring is often a one-size-fits-all technical exercise, but policy and supply chain interventions require answers to specific questions (e.g., conversion type, actor identity, legality). Pre-2026, the proliferation of change detection algorithms has outpaced systematic guidance on their alignment with distinct socio-ecological research questions, leading to potential mismatches between data and actionable evidence for sustainability governance.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, question-first framework that maps specific policy and ecological queries to optimized sequences of remote sensing techniques will yield more accurate, interpretable, and decision-relevant maps of land-cover change than applying generic change detection methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision-tree framework linking common questions in commodity-driven deforestation (e.g., 'Was deforestation illegal?', 'Did it lead to forest degradation or clearance?') to tailored analytical pipelines. For each node, we will test and integrate specific pre-2026 methods: deep learning segmentation (U-Net) for fine-scale land-cover classification, CCDC-based time-series analysis for disturbance timing, and object-based image analysis (OBIA) to link changes to farm or concession boundaries. Validation will use high-resolution imagery and ground-truth data from cocoa and palm oil landscapes in West Africa and Southeast Asia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-access toolkit for researchers and monitoring agencies to select technically appropriate and policy-relevant monitoring methods. It will advance the science of land-change measurement by formally embedding socio-legal and ecological questions into the remote sensing workflow, directly supporting the evaluation of deforestation-free supply chain commitments.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing methods are well-developed, the systematic translation of a policy question into a technical specification for change detection is novel. This moves beyond benchmarking algorithm accuracy to benchmarking the fitness-for-purpose of entire methodological chains within specific governance contexts.

proposals.title: Bridging scales in land system science: A hierarchical model coupling local value chain dynamics to global forest cover projections
proposals.motivation: Global land system models (e.g., CLUMondo, MAgPIE) project land-use change but often rely on coarse, aggregate drivers, missing critical sub-national heterogeneity in decision-making. Conversely, fine-scale empirical models of land manager behavior, informed by value chain analysis, capture local realities but lack capacity for large-area, long-term projection. This scale mismatch limits the credibility of policy-relevant forecasts for deforestation and sustainable commodity transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical modeling framework that statistically couples fine-scale empirical models of land-use decisions, parameterized by local value chain and policy factors, to the driver-response functions of a global dynamic ecosystem model will produce more accurate and spatially explicit projections of forest cover change than either model class alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tiered architecture. First, we will estimate local empirical models (e.g., agent-based or statistical models) for key commodity frontiers (e.g., cocoa in West Africa, soy in Cerrado) using high-resolution satellite data (Landsat/Sentinel-2) and field-survey data on farmer practices, value chain linkages, and policies. Second, we will use a Bayesian calibration approach to inform the land transition probabilities and elasticity parameters of a global model (e.g., the LandSHIFT model) with the posterior distributions from the local models, ensuring consistency across scales. Deep learning segmentation (U-Net architectures) will be employed to generate consistent, high-resolution land-cover time series for model training and validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable method for scale integration in land system science, moving beyond simple downscaling. It will deliver improved, policy-relevant projections of forest outcomes under different commodity governance scenarios. The research will directly inform the design of more effective, context-sensitive 'deforestation-free' supply chain interventions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale modeling is recognized as important, few studies formally couple empirical, value-chain-informed local models with global dynamic models in a statistically rigorous Bayesian framework. This approach explicitly bridges Lambin's work on local supply chain factors with global land-change modeling.

proposals.title: Modeling Land-Use Leakage and Scarcity in Global Commodity Trade: Integrating Biophysical Constraints into a Computable General Equilibrium Framework
proposals.motivation: Global supply chain policies like zero-deforestation commitments risk displacing environmental impacts to less-regulated regions, a process known as leakage. Pre-2026 macroeconomic trade models often treat land as a generic, substitutable factor, failing to capture the biophysical heterogeneity and scarcity that drive real-world land-use change. This gap limits our ability to forecast the true efficacy of sustainability governance in agricultural and forestry commodity trade.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating spatially explicit, biophysical land constraints—such as soil suitability, slope, and proximity to intact forests—into a global Computable General Equilibrium (CGE) model will reveal significantly higher rates of leakage and more severe trade-offs between agricultural production and conservation than standard economic models predict.
proposals.method: We will augment the GTAP (Global Trade Analysis Project) CGE framework by disaggregating the land endowment factor. Using pre-2026 global datasets (e.g., FAO's Global Agro-Ecological Zones, ESA CCI Land Cover), we will develop a nested Constant Elasticity of Transformation (CET) function where land is allocated between uses within biophysically similar clusters. The model will be calibrated to 2019 trade and land-use data. Scenarios will simulate demand shocks for deforestation-free commodities, with outcomes (production shifts, land rents, deforestation) compared against a baseline model with homogeneous land.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, policy-relevant tool for quantifying the indirect land-use effects of supply chain interventions. It will advance the field of land system science by formally coupling macroeconomic trade dynamics with fine-scale biophysical realities, offering more robust estimates of leakage. The findings will directly inform debates on the design of complementary demand- and supply-side policies to achieve genuine sustainability in global value chains.
proposals.novelty: While CGE models have incorporated land use, they rarely integrate spatially heterogeneous biophysical constraints that limit substitutability. This proposal uniquely bridges Lambin's expertise in value chain factors and remote-sensing-derived land systems with advanced economic modeling, moving beyond sectoral or country-level analyses to a spatially informed macroeconomic assessment.

ideas.title: Dual+: Participatory multi-objective control for navigating land system resilience trade-offs
ideas.description: This approach reframes land management as a participatory, multi-objective control problem. It begins by co-producing with stakeholders a set of quantifiable but often conflicting resilience objectives (e.g., biodiversity, agricultural yield, cultural value, carbon sequestration). A control-theoretic model of the land system is then used to map the Pareto-optimal frontier of management strategies, explicitly visualizing the trade-offs between different resilience goals under various future scenarios. The final output is not a single prescribed policy, but a decision-support tool that allows stakeholders to explore and negotiate the consequences of different choices, making the inherent value conflicts in resilience planning transparent and manageable.

ideas.title: A Functional Trait Matrix for Ecosystem State, Inspired by Materials Science
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the use of land cover class as the primary state variable in environmental modeling, proposing instead to use functional ecosystem traits or services as the core metric. The analogy is drawn from materials science, where a shift from classifying materials by type (e.g., metal, polymer) to characterizing them by functional properties (e.g., strength, conductivity) revolutionized design and engineering. Adapting this solution, the research would develop and implement a 'Functional Trait Matrix' for ecosystems, defining key quantifiable traits—such as carbon flux, hydrological function, biodiversity support, and surface energy balance—as the fundamental state variables. This approach would enable models to directly map and project ecosystem service provision, facilitating land management and policy decisions aimed at optimizing specific functional outcomes rather than merely managing categorical land cover labels.

ideas.title: Networked Archetypes of Land System Resilience: A Global Typology of Functional Connectivity
ideas.description: This research creates a global typology of land systems by upscaling dense local case studies, but uses ecological network analysis as the unifying analytical framework. Each local case is characterized as a network of land cover patches and flows (e.g., species, nutrients, people, capital), from which metrics of connectivity, modularity, and robustness are derived. These functional network metrics, rather than just land cover proportions, are then aggregated and clustered to define global archetypes of land system resilience. The result is a diagnostic map of the world that classifies regions by their dominant network regime, predicting their potential tipping points and capacity to sustain ecological functions under change.

ideas.title: A Scale-Aware Emulator Framework for Robust Land-Climate Feedback Integration
ideas.description: This research develops a modular integration framework to overcome the fundamental scale and process mismatch between land use change and climate models. It centers on creating a 'land system emulator'—a reduced-complexity statistical model—that translates high-resolution, socio-economic land change projections into their aggregate climate-relevant properties (e.g., albedo, roughness, carbon fluxes) at the appropriate grid scale for Earth System Models. The framework uses a flexible, uncertainty-quantifying coupler to manage the bidirectional exchange between the emulator and the climate model, enabling computationally tractable exploration of feedbacks while explicitly tracking the propagation of uncertainty from human decision-making through to climate outcomes.

ideas.title: Applying Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamics to Model Land System Fluxes
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the equilibrium assumption in land system science by drawing an analogy from non-equilibrium thermodynamics. In that field, systems far from equilibrium (e.g., living cells, weather patterns) are understood through the continuous flows of energy and matter that sustain their dynamic, often chaotic, structures—concepts like dissipative structures and entropy production. Adapting this solution, the research would investigate land systems as analogous dissipative structures, constantly shaped by socio-ecological fluxes. It would develop methods to quantify the major flows (of biomass, nutrients, capital, labor, information) that prevent the system from reaching a stable endpoint, model how these fluxes maintain specific dynamic regimes (e.g., intensive agriculture, urban sprawl), and identify thresholds where flux imbalances trigger systemic reorganization. This shifts the focus from predicting static land cover outcomes to diagnosing the metabolic processes of perpetual land system change.

ideas.title: Inverse Socio-Ecological State Estimation for Embedded Human-Environment Systems
ideas.description: This research develops inverse modeling frameworks that treat humans as fully embedded, stateful components within a land ecological system. Instead of inferring external socio-economic drivers, the models deduce the most probable internal system state—including human behavioral parameters, institutional rules, and ecological memory—from time series of satellite-observed land cover. This allows for diagnosing the system's current socio-ecological regime and resilience from pattern alone, providing a foundational tool for managing complex, coupled systems where humans and nature co-evolve.

ideas.title: Dynamic Land Cover Trajectories: Downscaling Non-Stationary Futures for Adaptive Local Planning
ideas.description: This research develops novel downscaling methods that explicitly incorporate the non-stationarity of land cover classifications driven by climate change and human activity. Instead of translating a single, stable future projection, the approach generates ensembles of parcel-level land cover trajectories, quantifying classification uncertainty and transition probabilities over time. This allows local decision-makers to stress-test policies (e.g., zoning, conservation investments, infrastructure development) against a range of plausible, dynamically changing landscape futures. The outcome is adaptive planning tools that are robust to the fundamental instability of land systems, moving beyond static downscaling to proactive scenario-based management.

ideas.title: Mapping Regime Dynamics and Critical Transitions in Land Systems
ideas.description: This research investigates land systems as complex systems characterized by multiple, alternative dynamic regimes rather than a single equilibrium or pure chaos. It seeks to empirically identify these persistent system states (e.g., different configurations of agricultural intensity, forest connectivity, or urban form) and the key processes that maintain them. The core focus is on modeling the thresholds and feedback loops that trigger critical transitions from one regime to another, using historical and contemporary data to detect early-warning signals. The outcome is a diagnostic framework that enables prediction of potential system shifts and informs management strategies aimed at either fostering resilience within a desired regime or facilitating a deliberate transition.

ideas.title: Apply Vector Autoregression and Network Analysis from Neuroscience to Land Teleconnections
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from systems neuroscience, where Vector Autoregression (VAR) modeling and network analysis are used to map directional influences (Granger causality) between multiple brain regions from fMRI time-series data. The analogous problem of understanding complex, delayed interactions within a network is solved by first fitting a multivariate VAR model to the entire system's time-series and then deriving a causality network from the model coefficients. Adapted to land system science, this involves treating major land-use types across the globe as interconnected nodes. By applying a global VAR model to longitudinal remote-sensing data (e.g., annual land-cover maps), we can model the simultaneous interactions within the global land system. The fitted model then allows for the construction of a comprehensive Granger-causal network, visually and quantitatively mapping how land changes in one region (e.g., deforestation) predictively 'cause' land changes in other, potentially distant regions, revealing the full architecture of land teleconnections.

ideas.title: Dynamic Causal Attribution of Natural Capital Impacts via Land Surface Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This research integrates data assimilation (DA) techniques from numerical weather prediction with causal modeling of land cover impacts. It develops a DA framework that ingests heterogeneous satellite and ground data (e.g., reflectance, LST, soil moisture, atmospheric CO2) to produce dynamically updated, high-resolution states of land surface attributes. These continuously corrected states are then used to drive and validate causal impact models, allowing for the detection of emergent natural capital impacts from recent land cover changes, the separation of these impacts from background climate variability, and the probabilistic attribution of observed biophysical changes to specific anthropogenic or natural drivers.

ideas.title: Adapting Power Grid Stability Metrics for Land System Network Resilience
ideas.description: This idea adapts network analysis techniques from electrical engineering, specifically power grid stability assessment, to analyze land system resilience. Power grids are modeled as networks where engineers use N-1 contingency testing, centrality metrics, and cascading failure simulations to identify critical components and systemic vulnerabilities. This solution is adapted by conceptualizing a land system as a network where land cover patches (e.g., forests, wetlands, agricultural fields) are nodes, and the flows of species, nutrients, water, or human resources between them are weighted edges. The adapted research would apply power grid analytical frameworks—such as simulating the removal of high-centrality patches, calculating load distribution across the network, and modeling tipping points for cascading land conversion—to quantify the resilience, identify critical leverage points, and predict failure scenarios in coupled human-natural land systems.

ideas.title: Modeling Land System Resilience via Ecological State-Space Control
ideas.description: This idea frames land management as an optimal control problem, directly analogous to how Model Predictive Control (MPC) is used in aerospace engineering and robotics to guide dynamic systems. The analogous solution involves using an internal model to predict future system states, solving a finite-horizon optimization for control inputs, and applying this in a rolling feedback loop. Adapted to land systems, the landscape's key resilience indicators (state variables) are modeled dynamically. A controller uses an ecological process model to predict how these states will evolve under potential management actions (control inputs). It repeatedly solves for the cost-minimizing sequence of actions that navigates the system toward a target resilient basin of attraction, subject to constraints, implementing the near-term actions before recalculating with updated observational data. This provides a rigorous, adaptive framework for operationalizing resilience in land management.

ideas.title: Observable Proxy-to-Function (OPF) framework for scalable ecosystem state mapping
ideas.description: This framework moves beyond land cover by establishing a rigorous, scalable link between directly observable remote sensing data and latent ecosystem functions. It uses a suite of physical and phenological proxies, derived from multi-sensor data, to drive process-based models that estimate core functional traits and services like productivity, water use, and habitat quality. A hierarchical Bayesian data assimilation system explicitly quantifies and communicates the uncertainty in these functional estimates. The output is a dynamic, probabilistic map of ecosystem functional states, providing a more ecologically meaningful metric for management while remaining operationally feasible for large areas.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery and Double ML Framework for Robust Identification of Land Use Change Drivers
ideas.description: This research develops a robust analytical framework that combines causal discovery algorithms with double machine learning to overcome the strong assumptions of standard econometric models. It first applies temporal and spatial causal discovery techniques to high-resolution land cover and socioeconomic panel data to data-adaptively identify plausible causal graphs and potential confounders. The framework then uses double/debiased machine learning to estimate the causal effects of key socioeconomic drivers, providing valid confidence intervals even with high-dimensional controls and nonlinear relationships. Finally, the inferred drivers and their effect sizes are rigorously validated through spatial out-of-sample prediction and placebo tests, ensuring the findings are not artifacts of local spatial autocorrelation or residual confounding.

ideas.title: Quantifying Land System Dynamics: Computer Vision for Non-Equilibrium Process Detection
ideas.description: This research fuses the theoretical lens of non-equilibrium land systems with advanced computer vision to develop a new analytical framework. Instead of simply detecting discrete land cover changes, the project trains convolutional neural networks to identify and quantify the ongoing processes of modification, adaptation, and resilience in satellite imagery time series. This allows for the mapping of system dynamics—such as rates of incremental transformation, sequences of land use adjustments, and spatial signatures of feedback loops—directly from observational data. The result is an empirical, process-oriented understanding of land systems as complex, adaptive entities without a stable equilibrium, providing critical insights for managing environmental change.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Infer drivers from observed land patterns

ideas.title: Modeling the Human-Environment System as a Holobiont
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a strict boundary between human systems and ecological systems by drawing an analogy from microbial ecology's 'holobiont' concept. The analogous solution treats a host and its microbiome as a single, co-evolved functional unit, analyzed through integrated network and metabolic models. The adapted research idea proposes modeling human-environment systems as 'land holobionts,' using integrated network or agent-based models to treat human and ecological components as nodes in a single system, connected by bidirectional flows. This approach aims to uncover emergent properties like systemic resilience and metabolic interdependencies that arise from the embedded, symbiotic relationships, moving beyond traditional external actor frameworks.

ideas.title: Apply resilience theory from ecology

ideas.title: A 'Paleoclimatology' Approach to Satellite Data: Cross-Calibration and Gap-Filling for a Continuous Land Record
ideas.description: This research adapts the core problem-solving approach from paleoclimatology—creating a unified, continuous record from fragmented and heterogeneous data sources—to the domain of global land monitoring. The analogous problem in paleoclimatology is solved by cross-calibrating disparate proxy records (like ice cores and tree rings) and using statistical models to fill gaps and resolve inconsistencies, yielding a single climate timeline. The adapted idea is to create a consistent, long-term, high-resolution global land data record by developing a novel computational framework. This framework would perform cross-sensor calibration and advanced statistical harmonization to merge data from the entire historical fleet of land-observing satellites (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel, MODIS). It would correct for inter-sensor differences and mitigate data gaps, ultimately producing a seamless, physically consistent, and analysis-ready time series for precise global land cover change detection over decades.

ideas.title: Dynamic Land-Use Forecasting via Data Assimilation for Parcel-Scale Decision Support
ideas.description: This research develops a novel framework that applies data assimilation techniques from numerical weather prediction to dynamically downscale continental-scale land-use and land-cover projections to the parcel level. The system continuously integrates heterogeneous, high-resolution data streams—such as from field sensors, UAVs, and satellite constellations—to correct and update the initial coarse model states and parameters. This produces a dynamically updated, probabilistic 'nowcast' and forecast of land surface attributes (e.g., soil moisture, vegetation health, development risk) tailored to individual land parcels. The result is a decision-support tool that provides land managers and planners with actionable, locally relevant projections that evolve with incoming data, significantly reducing the uncertainty gap between global models and on-the-ground choices.

ideas.title: Inferring Global Telecoupled Drivers from Land Cover Sequences via Causal Econometric Inversion
ideas.description: This research develops an inverse modeling framework that integrates high-resolution satellite land cover time-series with global socio-economic datasets. It employs time-series econometric methods, such as panel Granger causality and vector autoregression, within a formal inverse problem setup to deduce the most likely set of local and telecoupled global drivers responsible for observed land change sequences. The approach uniquely quantifies the direction, strength, and time lags of causal relationships, moving beyond pattern recognition to statistically test hypotheses about specific distant drivers. This enables the creation of 'attribution maps' that explain local land dynamics through a validated network of global economic and trade influences.

ideas.title: Scale up innovation connection to transnational networks

ideas.sourceTitle: Tipping Toward Sustainability: Emerging Pathways of Transformation

ideas.title: A Dynamical Systems and Robust Causality Framework for Land Teleconnection Analysis
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond standard Granger causality to a multi-method causal inference system for land teleconnections. It applies convergent cross-mapping to detect non-linear, potentially bidirectional causal links in time-series data while using panel econometric techniques to control for latent global confounders. The core innovation is a validation loop where hypothesized causal drivers, identified statistically, are tested within spatially explicit agent-based or land system models to assess their mechanistic sufficiency. This creates a robust, empirically grounded analysis that separates true teleconnections from spurious correlations and identifies the scale-dependent conditions under which they operate.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Contact Network Models to Simulate Information Diffusion in Land Use Decisions
ideas.description: This research adapts agent-based modeling (ABM) techniques from epidemiology to simulate decentralized decision-making in land use change. The analogous solution from epidemiology involves modeling disease spread through contact networks, where agents with specific states interact based on network topology and probabilistic rules, leading to emergent global infection patterns. This solution is adapted by treating land managers as agents whose 'infection' is the adoption of a new land use practice. Their 'contact network' is defined by social connections or spatial adjacency. The adapted ABM simulates how peer influence, information diffusion through this network, and heterogeneous agent characteristics interact to produce emergent, large-scale land use transitions, providing a mechanistic understanding of decentralized spatial decision dynamics.

ideas.title: A Global Synthesis Engine for Land System Archetypes Using Automated Satellite Image Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a computational pipeline that applies convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to continuously scan global, multi-temporal satellite imagery to automatically detect and catalog instances of land cover modification. Each detected case is embedded with spatially explicit contextual data on potential socio-economic and biophysical drivers. By systematically comparing this vast, self-assembled database of cases through machine learning clustering and pattern recognition, the project moves beyond single-case studies to inductively identify generalizable land system archetypes. This enables the formulation of robust, empirically grounded theories on the common pathways and causal mechanisms of land change worldwide.

ideas.title: Inverse Land-Use Attribution via Bayesian Source Separation
ideas.description: This research adapts the Bayesian component separation method from astrophysics, used to disentangle mixed signals like the CMB, to solve the inverse problem in land system science. Satellite-observed land cover sequences are treated as a composite signal generated by the superposition of multiple, unobserved socio-economic drivers. By developing an inverse model that incorporates prior knowledge about driver signatures (e.g., spatial patterns, temporal lags) and multi-spectral/time-series data, the research aims to probabilistically infer the most likely mix and intensity of underlying drivers—such as agricultural subsidies, urbanization policies, or commodity booms—responsible for any given observed land change pattern, moving from correlation to causal attribution.

ideas.title: Inferring Critical Land Attributes via Indirect Proxy Networks
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that all critical land surface attributes must be captured directly by satellite sensors in the visible, thermal, and microwave spectra. Drawing an analogy from paleoclimatology, where unobservable past climates are reconstructed from indirect proxy networks (e.g., tree rings, ice cores), the adapted idea proposes to solve the satellite data gap by identifying and validating a suite of indirect 'land attribute proxies.' The solution involves developing a statistical framework to infer essential but unmeasured attributes—such as soil microbial activity, specific subsurface mineralogy, or root zone chemical properties—by fusing subtle, correlated signatures across existing satellite data bands. This framework would be calibrated and validated against targeted, sparse ground-truth measurements, creating a method to derive critical land intelligence from existing sensor data without requiring new, direct-detection hardware for every parameter.

ideas.title: Weak-constraint hybrid data assimilation for land change models with online structural error correction
ideas.description: This research develops a novel data assimilation (DA) framework specifically designed for the structural inadequacies of land change models. It moves beyond traditional strong-constraint DA by formally estimating and correcting for model error within the assimilation cycle. The system combines a weak-constraint ensemble DA algorithm with an embedded machine learning model that learns the bias and error patterns from the ongoing stream of observational data. This allows for the robust integration of heterogeneous observations while dynamically compensating for the land change model's known deficiencies in representing human and ecological processes.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Hypothesis Testing for Land Change Attribution
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic attribution system that couples inverse inference with forward prediction to overcome the non-uniqueness of land change drivers. The system first uses an inverse model to generate a constrained ensemble of probable socio-economic driver combinations from historical land cover sequences. Each candidate driver set is then used to parameterize a mechanistic land change model, generating testable forecasts of future land patterns. These forecasts are continuously validated against a stream of new satellite observations, allowing the system to falsify incorrect driver hypotheses and iteratively refine the set of plausible explanations, moving from pure correlation to causal attribution.

ideas.title: Robust Local Decision Pathways Under Deeply Uncertain Continental Futures
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to bridge continental-scale projections and parcel-level decisions by explicitly managing the deep uncertainty in downscaling. It generates an ensemble of spatially coherent local scenarios that are all consistent with the coarse-scale projections, rather than a single deterministic map. The system then employs agent-based simulation to stress-test local decision strategies across this ensemble, identifying actions that are robust (perform acceptably across many futures) or adaptive (can be triggered by local monitoring), thereby providing actionable guidance despite irreducible uncertainty.

ideas.title: Dynamic Land System Assimilation via Vision-Informed Observational Priors
ideas.description: This research integrates computer vision-detected land modifications directly into a geophysical land change model using data assimilation (DA) frameworks from climate science. The CNN acts as a sophisticated, automated observation operator, converting raw pixel data into quantified modification signals (e.g., probability and type of change) that serve as high-frequency, spatially explicit inputs to the DA system. The DA framework optimally merges these heterogeneous vision-based observations with the model's physics-based predictions, dynamically correcting model states and parameters. This enables the model to not just retrospectively map changes, but to forecast the trajectory of fine-scale land system evolution by learning the underlying processes driving the modifications detected by the vision system.

ideas.title: A Generative, Physics-Informed Sensor Emulator for Fidelity-Preserving Global Land Change Detection
ideas.description: This project develops a novel AI-driven framework to create long-term, high-resolution land data records that prioritize geophysical fidelity over simple consistency. A core generative model, trained on overlapping satellite observations and constrained by radiative transfer physics, learns to emulate how different sensors would observe the same underlying land surface state. This allows all historical and current satellite data to be translated into a unified, physically meaningful representation without loss of unique signal information. The resulting synthetic data stream is inherently interoperable, maintains true geophysical trends, and significantly improves the detection of subtle, long-term land cover and condition changes.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Multi-Scale Data Assimilation Framework for Robust Land Surface Characterization
ideas.description: This research develops a computationally tractable, hybrid data assimilation system specifically designed for the high-dimensional, non-Gaussian parameter space of land surface models. It combines an ensemble-based filter for dynamically updating system states (like surface soil moisture and temperature) with a sequential Bayesian parameter estimation scheme for critical, slow-varying land attributes. The framework intelligently scales the assimilation process, using satellite data for frequent state corrections and integrating heterogeneous ground observations to continuously refine model parameters and structural uncertainties. This approach overcomes the curse of dimensionality to provide robust, physically consistent estimates of land surface fluxes and storages across scales.

ideas.title: A Super-Resolution Framework for Satellite Data Fusion Using Convolutional Neural Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from medical imaging—single-image super-resolution using convolutional neural networks (CNNs)—to the problem of integrating fine and coarse resolution satellite data. In the analogous field, CNNs are trained on paired low- and high-resolution images (e.g., MRI scans) to learn a mapping that can predict high-resolution details from a low-resolution input. The adapted idea applies this to satellite remote sensing by training a CNN model on temporally and atmospherically matched pairs of coarse-resolution (high-frequency) and fine-resolution (low-frequency) satellite imagery. The trained model can then be applied to the frequent, coarse-resolution data streams (e.g., MODIS, VIIRS) to generate synthetic imagery at a finer spatial resolution, effectively fusing the datasets to produce a continuous, high-quality time series for improved land cover change detection, phenology monitoring, and ecosystem assessment.

ideas.title: Apply Change Detection CNNs from Medical Imaging to Satellite Land Modification Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in medical imaging—detecting subtle anatomical changes in sequential scans—to the domain of satellite imagery analysis. In radiology, convolutional neural networks are successfully used for longitudinal change detection, such as tracking lesion progression between two MRI scans. The analogous solution involves architectures that take two temporally separated images as input to produce a precise change map. Adapted to the research context, this approach would employ a similar CNN model (e.g., a Siamese U-Net) trained on pairs of multi-spectral satellite images from different dates. The model would learn to identify and highlight pixels representing significant land cover modifications like deforestation or construction, while ignoring irrelevant changes due to seasons, weather, or sensor artifacts, thereby providing a powerful tool for environmental monitoring, urban planning, and illegal activity detection.

ideas.title: Employ Ensemble Kalman Filtering from Seismic Reservoir Characterization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a data assimilation solution from the field of geophysics, specifically seismic reservoir characterization, where the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) is used to merge indirect seismic data with direct well logs and physical models to estimate subsurface properties. The analogous solution involves maintaining an ensemble of possible models, forward simulating them, and statistically correcting them with incoming data to reduce uncertainty. Adapted to land surface science, this approach would use EnKF to integrate heterogeneous ground-based measurements (e.g., soil moisture probes, flux towers) and satellite-derived observations (e.g., brightness temperatures, NDVI) into a dynamic land surface model. The result is a continuously updated, high-resolution, and physically consistent estimate of key land surface attributes with quantified uncertainties, directly analogous to how reservoir models are updated with seismic and production data.

ideas.title: Dynamic Context-Aware Fusion of Multi-Resolution Satellite Data Using Physics-Guided Self-Supervision
ideas.description: This research develops a fusion framework that dynamically models the non-stationary relationship between high-spatial and high-temporal resolution satellite sensors. Instead of a static fusion algorithm, it uses a physics-informed neural network trained with self-supervision, where radiative transfer simulations and atmospheric data provide constraints to learn how spectral and spatial responses change with sun angle, aerosols, and phenology. The system continuously validates its output against a network of pseudo-invariant features and enforces temporal coherence, producing a robust, bias-corrected, high-resolution time series specifically designed for reliable change detection and land cover monitoring.
